nodes.name: Addition on a Peano System
nodes.question: State the definition: Addition on a Peano System.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For each $m \in P$, define the function
\[
f_m : P \to P
\]
to be the unique function satisfying
\[
f_m(1)=S(m)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(f_m(S(n))=S(f_m(n))).
\]
For $m,n \in P$, define
\[
m+n \coloneqq f_m(n).
\]

nodes.failure_modes:
The definition fails only when the designated value does not agree with the
unique recursively generated function attached to the fixed left input $m$.
Once the recursive map $f_m$ is fixed, every incorrect candidate for $m+n$ is
simply a value of $P$ different from $f_m(n)$.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system. For each } m \in P,
\text{ let } f_m : P \to P \text{ satisfy}\\
&f_m(1)=S(m)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(f_m(S(n))=S(f_m(n))).\\
&x \neq m+n
\Longleftrightarrow
\underbrace{x \neq f_m(n)}_{\text{candidate value disagrees with the recursive output}}.
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.source_text:
Addition is introduced by fixing the left input and recursively generating the
right-input map. The value $m+1$ is set to be the successor of $m$, and each
further successor step in the right input advances the output by one more
successor. This makes addition repeated succession.

nodes.name: Addition Is Well-Defined on a Peano System
nodes.question: State the theorem: Addition Is Well-Defined on a Peano System.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m \in P$, there exists a unique
function
\[
f_m : P \to P
\]
such that
\[
f_m(1)=S(m)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(f_m(S(n))=S(f_m(n))).
\]
Consequently the rule
\[
(m,n) \longmapsto m+n \coloneqq f_m(n)
\]
defines a unique binary operation on $P$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m \in P$, there exists a unique
function
\[
f_m : P \to P
\]
such that
\[
f_m(1)=S(m)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(f_m(S(n))=S(f_m(n))).
\]
Consequently the rule
\[
(m,n) \longmapsto m+n \coloneqq f_m(n)
\]
defines a unique binary operation on $P$.
nodes.source_text:
The theorem shows that the recursive clauses for addition determine exactly one
right-input map for each fixed left input. The addition operation is therefore
not assumed; it is constructed from the successor map by recursion.

nodes.name: Addition With $1$
nodes.question: State the theorem: Addition With $1$.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m \in P$,
\[
m+1=S(m).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m \in P$,
\[
m+1=S(m).
\]
nodes.source_text:
This theorem records the base clause of the recursive definition in the
operation notation itself. Adding the distinguished first element applies one
successor step to the left input.

nodes.name: Addition Successor on the Right
nodes.question: State the theorem: Addition Successor on the Right.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m,n \in P$,
\[
m+S(n)=S(m+n).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m,n \in P$,
\[
m+S(n)=S(m+n).
\]
nodes.source_text:
This theorem records the recursive step of addition in operation notation.
Moving one successor step forward in the right input moves the sum one
successor step forward as well.

nodes.name: One Plus $n$
nodes.question: State the theorem: One Plus $n$.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $n \in P$,
\[
1+n=S(n).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $n \in P$,
\[
1+n=S(n).
\]
nodes.source_text:
The theorem shows that the distinguished first element acts on either side as
a single successor step. This is the first sign that the recursively defined
operation is not tied permanently to the asymmetry of its definition.

nodes.name: Addition Is Associative
nodes.question: State the theorem: Addition Is Associative.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
(a+b)+c=a+(b+c).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
(a+b)+c=a+(b+c).
\]
nodes.source_text:
Associativity shows that successive additions can be regrouped without
changing the value. This makes longer sums structurally coherent and prepares
addition for later interaction with order and multiplication.

nodes.name: Addition Is Commutative
nodes.question: State the theorem: Addition Is Commutative.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$,
\[
a+b=b+a.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$,
\[
a+b=b+a.
\]
nodes.source_text:
Although addition is defined by recursion on the right input, the resulting
operation is symmetric in its two arguments. Commutativity shows that the
apparent asymmetry of the defining clauses is an artifact of construction
rather than a feature of the operation itself.

nodes.name: Left Cancellation for Addition
nodes.question: State the theorem: Left Cancellation for Addition.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a+b=a+c
\Longrightarrow
b=c.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a+b=a+c
\Longrightarrow
b=c.
\]
nodes.source_text:
Left cancellation shows that adding the same element to two inputs cannot
erase a difference between them. This is the algebraic core that later makes
additive definitions of order behave well.

nodes.name: Right Cancellation for Addition
nodes.question: State the theorem: Right Cancellation for Addition.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
b+a=c+a
\Longrightarrow
b=c.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
b+a=c+a
\Longrightarrow
b=c.
\]
nodes.source_text:
Right cancellation is the companion to left cancellation. Once commutativity
is known, the placement of the common summand no longer matters, so equality
can be tested after adding the same element on either side.

nodes.name: $ $ Is Closed, Associative, and Commutative Under Addition
nodes.question: State the theorem: $ $ Is Closed, Associative, and Commutative Under Addition.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. The binary operation $+$ on $P$ satisfies:
- \textbf{Closure.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a+b \in P$.
- \textbf{Associativity.} For all $a,b,c \in P$,
 $(a+b)+c = a+(b+c)$.
- \textbf{Commutativity.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a+b = b+a$.
- \textbf{Left cancellation.} For all $a,b,c \in P$, if $a+b = a+c$ then $b=c$.
- \textbf{Right cancellation.} For all $a,b,c \in P$, if $b+a = c+a$ then $b=c$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. The binary operation $+$ on $P$ satisfies:
\begin{enumerate}
 \item \textbf{Closure.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a+b \in P$.
 \item \textbf{Associativity.} For all $a,b,c \in P$,
 $(a+b)+c = a+(b+c)$.
 \item \textbf{Commutativity.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a+b = b+a$.
 \item \textbf{Left cancellation.} For all $a,b,c \in P$, if $a+b = a+c$ then $b=c$.
 \item \textbf{Right cancellation.} For all $a,b,c \in P$, if $b+a = c+a$ then $b=c$.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.source_text:
This theorem is a packaging result. It names the algebraic structure that
has been proved piece by piece: the three individual theorems on associativity,
commutativity, and cancellation together certify that $(P,+)$ is a cancellative
commutative semigroup. There is no additive identity in $P$ because $P$ has
no element $0$ with $0+n=n$ for all $n$; the smallest element under addition
is $1$, not an identity.

nodes.name: $ $ Is a Commutative Monoid Under Multiplication
nodes.question: State the theorem: $ $ Is a Commutative Monoid Under Multiplication.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. The binary operation $\cdot$ on $P$ satisfies:
- \textbf{Closure.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a \cdot b \in P$.
- \textbf{Associativity.} For all $a,b,c \in P$,
 $(a \cdot b) \cdot c = a \cdot (b \cdot c)$.
- \textbf{Commutativity.} For all $a,b \in P$,
 $a \cdot b = b \cdot a$.
- \textbf{Identity.} For all $a \in P$,
 $1 \cdot a = a = a \cdot 1$.
Consequently $(P, \cdot, 1)$ is a commutative monoid.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. The binary operation $\cdot$ on $P$ satisfies:
\begin{enumerate}
 \item \textbf{Closure.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a \cdot b \in P$.
 \item \textbf{Associativity.} For all $a,b,c \in P$,
 $(a \cdot b) \cdot c = a \cdot (b \cdot c)$.
 \item \textbf{Commutativity.} For all $a,b \in P$,
 $a \cdot b = b \cdot a$.
 \item \textbf{Identity.} For all $a \in P$,
 $1 \cdot a = a = a \cdot 1$.
\end{enumerate}
Consequently $(P, \cdot, 1)$ is a commutative monoid.
nodes.source_text:
The distinguished first element $1$ is a multiplicative identity on both
sides, so multiplication promotes $P$ from a semigroup to a monoid. The
absence of multiplicative inverses (other than $1^{-1} = 1$, which does not
lie in $P$ in the usual sense) is what prevents $(P,\cdot)$ from being a
group. The structural limitation is intrinsic: no element of $P$ other than
$1$ has a reciprocal in $P$.

nodes.name: $ $ Is a Semiring
nodes.question: State the theorem: $ $ Is a Semiring.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. Then $(P,+,\cdot)$ satisfies:
- $(P,+)$ is a commutative semigroup.
- $(P,\cdot,1)$ is a commutative monoid.
- Multiplication distributes over addition: for all $a,b,c \in P$,
 \[
 a \cdot (b+c) = (a \cdot b) + (a \cdot c).
 \]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. Then $(P,+,\cdot)$ satisfies:
\begin{enumerate}
 \item $(P,+)$ is a commutative semigroup.
 \item $(P,\cdot,1)$ is a commutative monoid.
 \item Multiplication distributes over addition: for all $a,b,c \in P$,
 \[
 a \cdot (b+c) = (a \cdot b) + (a \cdot c).
 \]
\end{enumerate}
nodes.source_text:
A semiring packages the interaction of two operations without requiring
additive inverses. The natural numbers are the canonical example of a semiring
that is not a ring: they satisfy every ring axiom except the existence of
additive inverses, which fails because there is no element $-1$ in $P$.
The integers are the smallest ring extending $P$ that repairs this failure.

nodes.name: Tao 2.2.11 — Order on $ $
nodes.question: State the definition: Tao 2.2.11 — Order on $ $.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $n, m \in \mathbb{N}$.
\[
n \geq m \quad (\text{equivalently } m \leq n)
\quad \iff \quad
\exists\, a \in \mathbb{N},\; n = m + a.
\]
\[
n > m \quad (\text{equivalently } m < n)
\quad \iff \quad
n \geq m \text{ and } n \neq m.
\]

nodes.name: Tao 2.2.12 — Basic properties of order
nodes.question: State the proposition: Tao 2.2.12 — Basic properties of order.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$.
- \textbf{Reflexive.} $a \geq a$.
- \textbf{Transitive.} If $a \geq b$ and $b \geq c$, then $a \geq c$.
- \textbf{Anti-symmetric.} If $a \geq b$ and $b \geq a$, then $a = b$.
- \textbf{Addition preserves order.}
 $a \geq b \iff a + c \geq b + c$.
- $a < b \iff a\pp \leq b$.
- $a < b \iff b = a + d$ for some positive $d$.

nodes.name: Exponentiation — Tao 2.3.11
nodes.question: State the definition: Exponentiation — Tao 2.3.11.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $m \in \mathbb{N}$. Exponentiation is defined recursively:
\[
m^0 := 1, \qquad m^{n\pp} := m^n \times m.
\]
In particular, $0^0 := 1$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $m \in \mathbb{N}$. Exponentiation is defined recursively:
\[
m^0 := 1, \qquad m^{n\pp} := m^n \times m.
\]
In particular, $0^0 := 1$.
nodes.text_preview: [Exponentiation — Tao 2.3.11] Let $m $. Exponentiation is defined recursively: \[ m^0 := 1, m^ n := m^n m. \] In particular, $0^0 := 1$.

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.2 — Commutativity
nodes.question: State the lemma: Tao 2.3.2 — Commutativity.
nodes.statement_display: For all $n, m \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $n \times m = m \times n$.
nodes.statement_tex: For all $n, m \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $n \times m = m \times n$.
nodes.text_preview: [Tao 2.3.2 — Commutativity] For all $n, m $, $n m = m n$.

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.3 — No zero divisors
nodes.question: State the lemma: Tao 2.3.3 — No zero divisors.
nodes.statement_display:
For all $n, m \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $n \times m = 0$ if and only if
$n = 0$ or $m = 0$.

In particular, if $n$ and $m$ are both positive, then $nm$ is positive.

In particular, if $n$ and $m$ are both positive, then $nm$ is positive.

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.4 — Distributive law
nodes.question: State the proposition: Tao 2.3.4 — Distributive law.
nodes.statement_display:
For all $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$:
\[
a(b+c) = ab + ac \qquad \text{and} \qquad (b+c)a = ba + ca.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
For all $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$:
\[
a(b+c) = ab + ac \qquad \text{and} \qquad (b+c)a = ba + ca.
\]

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.5 — Associativity
nodes.question: State the proposition: Tao 2.3.5 — Associativity.
nodes.statement_display: For all $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $(a \times b) \times c = a \times (b \times c)$.
nodes.statement_tex: For all $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $(a \times b) \times c = a \times (b \times c)$.
nodes.text_preview: [Tao 2.3.5 — Associativity] For all $a, b, c $, $(a b) c = a (b c)$.

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.6 — Multiplication preserves order
nodes.question: State the proposition: Tao 2.3.6 — Multiplication preserves order.
nodes.statement_display: If $a < b$ and $c$ is positive, then $ac < bc$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $a < b$ and $c$ is positive, then $ac < bc$.

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.7 — Cancellation for multiplication
nodes.question: State the corollary: Tao 2.3.7 — Cancellation for multiplication.
nodes.statement_display: Let $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$ with $ac = bc$ and $c \neq 0$. Then $a = b$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$ with $ac = bc$ and $c \neq 0$. Then $a = b$.
nodes.text_preview: [Tao 2.3.7 — Cancellation for multiplication] Let $a, b, c $ with $ac = bc$ and $c 0$. Then $a = b$.

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.9 — Euclidean algorithm
nodes.question: State the proposition: Tao 2.3.9 — Euclidean algorithm.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $n \in \mathbb{N}$ and let $q$ be positive. Then there exist
$m, r \in \mathbb{N}$ such that:
\[
0 \leq r < q \qquad \text{and} \qquad n = mq + r.
\]

\]

nodes.name: Peano System
nodes.question: State the definition: Peano System.
nodes.statement_display:
A triple $(P,S,1)$ is called a \emph{Peano system} exactly when $P$ is a set,
$1 \in P$, $S : P \to P$ is a function, and the following axioms hold:
- $1 \in P$.
- For every $n \in P$, $S(n) \in P$.
- For every $n \in P$, $S(n) \neq 1$.
- For all $m,n \in P$, if $S(m)=S(n)$ then $m=n$.
- If $A \subseteq P$, $1 \in A$, and
 \[
 \forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A),
 \]
 then $A=P$.
nodes.statement_tex:
A triple $(P,S,1)$ is called a \emph{Peano system} exactly when $P$ is a set,
$1 \in P$, $S : P \to P$ is a function, and the following axioms hold:
\begin{enumerate}
 \item[\textbf{P1.}] $1 \in P$.
 \item[\textbf{P2.}] For every $n \in P$, $S(n) \in P$.
 \item[\textbf{P3.}] For every $n \in P$, $S(n) \neq 1$.
 \item[\textbf{P4.}] For all $m,n \in P$, if $S(m)=S(n)$ then $m=n$.
 \item[\textbf{P5.}] If $A \subseteq P$, $1 \in A$, and
 \[
 \forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A),
 \]
 then $A=P$.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&(P,S,1) \text{ is a Peano system}\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\Bigl(P \text{ is a set} \land 1 \in P \land S : P \to P \\
&\qquad \land \forall n \in P\;(S(n) \in P) \\
&\qquad \land \forall n \in P\;(S(n) \neq 1) \\
&\qquad \land \forall m,n \in P\;(S(m)=S(n) \Longrightarrow m=n) \\
&\qquad \land \forall A \subseteq P\;
\bigl((1 \in A \land
\forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A))
\Longrightarrow A=P\bigr)\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&(P,S,1) \text{ is a Peano system}\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\Bigl(P \text{ is a set} \land 1 \in P \land S : P \to P \\
&\qquad \land \forall n \in P\;(S(n) \in P) \\
&\qquad \land \forall n \in P\;(S(n) \neq 1) \\
&\qquad \land \forall m,n \in P\;(S(m)=S(n) \Longrightarrow m=n) \\
&\qquad \land \forall A \subseteq P\;
\bigl((1 \in A \land
\forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A))
\Longrightarrow A=P\bigr)\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.failure_modes:
A triple $(P,S,1)$ fails to be a Peano system if the distinguished element
does not lie in the underlying set, if the successor map leaves the set, if
the successor hits $1$, if the successor identifies two distinct elements, or
if there exists a proper subset of $P$ that contains $1$ and is closed under
successor.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\neg\,\text{$(P,S,1)$ is a Peano system}
\Longrightarrow\;&
1 \notin P \\
&\lor \exists n \in P\;(S(n) \notin P) \\
&\lor \exists n \in P\;(S(n)=1) \\
&\lor \exists m,n \in P\;(m \neq n \land S(m)=S(n)) \\
&\lor \exists A \subseteq P\;
\bigl(1 \in A \land
\forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A)
\land A \neq P\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.source_text:
A Peano system is the abstract data of counting: an underlying set, a first
element, and a successor operation satisfying the usual non-collision and
induction principles. The purpose of the definition is to isolate the ambient
object before the individual axioms are recorded and used one by one.

nodes.name: Distinguished Element Lies in the Underlying Set
nodes.question: State the axiom: Distinguished Element Lies in the Underlying Set.
nodes.source_text:
The counting system begins from a distinguished first element of the
underlying set. This is the base point from which the remaining elements are
generated by the successor operation.
used_by_titles: Every Element Is Either $1$ or a Successor
nodes.text_preview: [Distinguished Element Lies in the Underlying Set] \[ 1 P. \]

nodes.name: Closure Under Successor
nodes.question: State the axiom: Closure Under Successor.
nodes.statement_display: If $n \in P$, then $S(n) \in P$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $n \in P$, then $S(n) \in P$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall n \in P\;(S(n) \in P).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall n \in P\;(S(n) \in P).
\]
nodes.source_text:
Applying the successor operation to an element of the underlying set never
leaves that set. Successor is therefore an internal operation on the counting
system.
used_by_titles: Every Element Is Either $1$ or a Successor
nodes.text_preview: [Closure Under Successor] If $n P$, then $S(n) P$.

nodes.name: Distinguished Element Is Not a Successor
nodes.question: State the axiom: Distinguished Element Is Not a Successor.
nodes.statement_display: For every $n \in P$, $S(n) \neq 1$.
nodes.statement_tex: For every $n \in P$, $S(n) \neq 1$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall n \in P\;(S(n) \neq 1).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall n \in P\;(S(n) \neq 1).
\]
nodes.source_text:
The first natural number does not arise by taking the successor of an earlier
natural number. This keeps the counting system from folding backward onto its
starting point.
used_by_titles: The Distinguished Element Is the Unique Non-Successor
No Object Is Its Own Successor
nodes.text_preview: [Distinguished Element Is Not a Successor] For every $n P$, $S(n) 1$.

nodes.name: Injectivity of Successor
nodes.question: State the axiom: Injectivity of Successor.
nodes.statement_display: If $m,n \in P$ and $S(m)=S(n)$, then $m=n$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $m,n \in P$ and $S(m)=S(n)$, then $m=n$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall m,n \in P\;(S(m)=S(n) \Longrightarrow m=n).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall m,n \in P\;(S(m)=S(n) \Longrightarrow m=n).
\]
nodes.source_text:
Different natural numbers do not collapse to the same successor. The successor
operation preserves distinctness and lets predecessor reasoning work whenever a
successor is known.
used_by_titles: No Object Is Its Own Successor
Predecessor Existence and Uniqueness Away from $1$

nodes.name: Induction Axiom
nodes.question: State the axiom: Induction Axiom.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq P$. If $1 \in A$ and if
\[
\forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A),
\]
then $A=P$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $A \subseteq P$. If $1 \in A$ and if
\[
\forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A),
\]
then $A=P$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall A \subseteq P\;
\Bigl(\bigl(1 \in A \land
\forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A)\bigr)
\Longrightarrow A=P\Bigr).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall A \subseteq P\;
\Bigl(\bigl(1 \in A \land
\forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A)\bigr)
\Longrightarrow A=P\Bigr).
\]
nodes.source_text:
Any subset of the underlying set that contains the distinguished element and is
closed under successor must already contain the entire set. This axiom rules
out rogue elements and is the logical source of mathematical induction.
used_by_titles: Induction Principle for a Peano System
nodes.text_preview: [Induction Axiom] Let $A P$. If $1 A$ and if \[ n P\;(n A S(n) A), \] then $A=P$.

nodes.name: Induction Principle for a Peano System
nodes.question: State the theorem: Induction Principle for a Peano System.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $\varphi$ be a predicate on $P$.
If
\[
\varphi(1)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(\varphi(n) \Longrightarrow \varphi(S(n))),
\]
then
\[
\forall n \in P\;\varphi(n).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $\varphi$ be a predicate on $P$.
If
\[
\varphi(1)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(\varphi(n) \Longrightarrow \varphi(S(n))),
\]
then
\[
\forall n \in P\;\varphi(n).
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } \varphi
\text{ be a predicate on } P.\\
&\Bigl(\varphi(1) \land
\forall n \in P\;(\varphi(n) \Longrightarrow \varphi(S(n)))\Bigr)
\Longrightarrow
\forall n \in P\;\varphi(n).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } \varphi
\text{ be a predicate on } P.\\
&\Bigl(\varphi(1) \land
\forall n \in P\;(\varphi(n) \Longrightarrow \varphi(S(n)))\Bigr)
\Longrightarrow
\forall n \in P\;\varphi(n).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.contrapositive:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } \varphi
\text{ be a predicate on } P.\\
&\neg\forall n \in P\;\varphi(n)
\Longrightarrow
\neg\Bigl(\varphi(1) \land
\forall n \in P\;(\varphi(n) \Longrightarrow \varphi(S(n)))\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
Step 1. Build the subset that corresponds to the predicate.

The induction axiom in the definition of a Peano system is stated for subsets
of the underlying set $P$, while the theorem we want is stated for a
predicate $\varphi$ on $P$. To connect these two forms, define
\[
A:=\{n \in P : \varphi(n)\}.
\]
So $A$ is exactly the set of elements of $P$ for which the property
$\varphi$ holds.
Step 2. Check the base case in subset language.

We are given that
\[
\varphi(1)
\]
holds. By the definition of $A$, that means precisely that
\[
1 \in A.
\]
So the first hypothesis required by the induction axiom is satisfied.
Step 3. Check closure under successor in subset language.

Now let $n \in P$ and assume that
\[
n \in A.
\]
By the definition of $A$, this means that
\[
\varphi(n)
\]
holds. But the theorem also assumes that
\[
\forall n \in P\;(\varphi(n) \Longrightarrow \varphi(S(n))).
\]
Therefore $\varphi(S(n))$ holds, and so again by the definition of $A$ we get
\[
S(n) \in A.
\]
Since $n \in P$ was arbitrary, we have shown that
\[
\forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A).
\]
Thus $A$ is closed under the successor operation.
Step 4. Apply the induction axiom of the Peano system.

We have now proved both hypotheses needed for
\hyperref[ax:peano-induction]{Axiom~\ref*{ax:peano-induction}}:
\[
1 \in A
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A).
\]
Because $(P,S,1)$ is a Peano system, the induction axiom tells us that
\[
A=P.
\]
Step 5. Translate back to the predicate form.

To prove the desired conclusion, let $n \in P$. Since $A=P$, we must have
\[
n \in A.
\]
By the definition of $A$, this means that $\varphi(n)$ holds.

Because $n \in P$ was arbitrary, we conclude that
\[
\forall n \in P\;\varphi(n).
\]

\textbf{Conclusion.} The subset-form induction axiom implies the usual
predicate-form induction principle.
proof_steps:
Step 1. Build the subset that corresponds to the predicate.

The induction axiom in the definition of a Peano system is stated for subsets
of the underlying set $P$, while the theorem we want is stated for a
predicate $\varphi$ on $P$. To connect these two forms, define
\[
A:=\{n \in P : \varphi(n)\}.
\]
So $A$ is exactly the set of elements of $P$ for which the property
$\varphi$ holds.

Step 2. Check the base case in subset language.

We are given that
\[
\varphi(1)
\]
holds. By the definition of $A$, that means precisely that
\[
1 \in A.
\]
So the first hypothesis required by the induction axiom is satisfied.

Step 3. Check closure under successor in subset language.

Now let $n \in P$ and assume that
\[
n \in A.
\]
By the definition of $A$, this means that
\[
\varphi(n)
\]
holds. But the theorem also assumes that
\[
\forall n \in P\;(\varphi(n) \Longrightarrow \varphi(S(n))).
\]
Therefore $\varphi(S(n))$ holds, and so again by the definition of $A$ we get
\[
S(n) \in A.
\]
Since $n \in P$ was arbitrary, we have shown that
\[
\forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A).
\]
Thus $A$ is closed under the successor operation.

Step 4. Apply the induction axiom of the Peano system.

We have now proved both hypotheses needed for
\hyperref[ax:peano-induction]{Axiom~\ref*{ax:peano-induction}}:
\[
1 \in A
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A).
\]
Because $(P,S,1)$ is a Peano system, the induction axiom tells us that
\[
A=P.
\]

Step 5. Translate back to the predicate form.

To prove the desired conclusion, let $n \in P$. Since $A=P$, we must have
\[
n \in A.
\]
By the definition of $A$, this means that $\varphi(n)$ holds.

Because $n \in P$ was arbitrary, we conclude that
\[
\forall n \in P\;\varphi(n).
\]

\textbf{Conclusion.} The subset-form induction axiom implies the usual
predicate-form induction principle.
nodes.proof_idea:
The proof converts the predicate $\varphi$ into the subset
\[
A=\{n \in P : \varphi(n)\}.
\]
The base case $\varphi(1)$ becomes the statement $1 \in A$, and the
successor step for $\varphi$ becomes closure of $A$ under the successor map.
Once these two facts are established, the induction axiom for the Peano
system forces $A=P$. Translating back from membership in $A$ to truth of
$\varphi$ yields the predicate-form induction principle.
nodes.source_text:
This theorem converts the subset form of induction into the predicate form
used in ordinary arithmetic arguments. A property propagates through the
entire Peano system once it holds at the distinguished first element and is
preserved by successor. The theorem therefore turns the structural induction
axiom into a proof principle for arbitrary predicates on $P$.

No Object Is Its Own Successor
Strong Induction on a Peano System

nodes.name: Successor-Closed Subset
nodes.question: State the definition: Successor-Closed Subset.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $A \subseteq P$. The subset $A$ is
called \emph{successor-closed} exactly when
\[
\forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $A \subseteq P$. The subset $A$ is
called \emph{successor-closed} exactly when
\[
\forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A).
\]
nodes.failure_modes:
A subset fails to be successor-closed exactly when it contains some element of
$P$ but fails to contain that element's successor.
nodes.source_text:
A successor-closed subset is stable under one step of counting. Once an
element of $A$ is present, its successor remains in $A$ as well.
used_by_titles: Inductive Subset of a Peano System
nodes.text_preview: [Successor-Closed Subset] Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $A P$. The subset $A$ is called exactly when \[ n P\;(n A S(n) A). \]

nodes.name: Inductive Subset of a Peano System
nodes.question: State the definition: Inductive Subset of a Peano System.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $A \subseteq P$. The subset $A$ is
called \emph{inductive} exactly when $1 \in A$ and $A$ is successor-closed.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $A \subseteq P$. The subset $A$ is
called \emph{inductive} exactly when $1 \in A$ and $A$ is successor-closed.
nodes.failure_modes:
A subset fails to be inductive in exactly two ways: it may fail to contain the
distinguished first element, or it may contain some element while failing to
contain that element's successor.
nodes.source_text:
An inductive subset contains the distinguished first element and is closed
under successor. Such a subset contains every stage obtained by starting at
$1$ and applying successor repeatedly.
nodes.text_preview: [Inductive Subset of a Peano System] Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $A P$. The subset $A$ is called exactly when $1 A$ and $A$ is successor-closed.

nodes.name: Predecessor in a Peano System
nodes.question: State the definition: Predecessor in a Peano System.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $x,u \in P$. The element $u$ is
called a \emph{predecessor} of $x$ exactly when
\[
S(u)=x.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $x,u \in P$. The element $u$ is
called a \emph{predecessor} of $x$ exactly when
\[
S(u)=x.
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } x,u \in P.\\
&u \text{ is a predecessor of } x
\Longleftrightarrow
S(u)=x.
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } x,u \in P.\\
&u \text{ is a predecessor of } x
\Longleftrightarrow
S(u)=x.
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } x,u \in P.\\
&u \text{ is not a predecessor of } x
\Longleftrightarrow
S(u) \neq x.
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } x,u \in P.\\
&u \text{ is not a predecessor of } x
\Longleftrightarrow
S(u) \neq x.
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.source_text:
A predecessor of $x$ is an element whose successor is $x$. This language
isolates the inverse-looking question attached to the successor map without
assuming that successor is globally invertible.
nodes.text_preview: [Predecessor in a Peano System] Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $x,u P$. The element $u$ is called a of $x$ exactly when \[ S(u)=x. \]

nodes.name: Every Element Is Either $1$ or a Successor
nodes.question: State the theorem: Every Element Is Either $1$ or a Successor.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $x \in P$, either $x=1$ or there
exists $u \in P$ such that $S(u)=x$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $x \in P$, either $x=1$ or there
exists $u \in P$ such that $S(u)=x$.
nodes.source_text:
Every element of a Peano system is reached by starting at the distinguished
first element and moving forward by successor. The only possible exception to
having a predecessor is the element $1$ itself.

Induction Principle for a Peano System

nodes.name: Predecessor Existence and Uniqueness Away from $1$
nodes.question: State the theorem: Predecessor Existence and Uniqueness Away from $1$.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $x \in P$ with $x \neq 1$. Then
there exists a unique $u \in P$ such that $S(u)=x$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $x \in P$ with $x \neq 1$. Then
there exists a unique $u \in P$ such that $S(u)=x$.
nodes.source_text:
Every element distinct from $1$ has exactly one predecessor. The successor map
therefore organizes the non-initial part of a Peano system into one-step
extensions of earlier elements.
depends_on_titles: Every Element Is Either $1$ or a Successor
used_by_titles: The Distinguished Element Is the Unique Non-Successor

nodes.text_preview: [Predecessor Existence and Uniqueness Away from $1$] Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $x P$ with $x 1$. Then there exists a unique $u P$ such that $S(u)=x$.

nodes.name: The Distinguished Element Is the Unique Non-Successor
nodes.question: State the corollary: The Distinguished Element Is the Unique Non-Successor.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. An element $x \in P$ is not a successor of
any element of $P$ if and only if $x=1$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. An element $x \in P$ is not a successor of
any element of $P$ if and only if $x=1$.
nodes.source_text:
The distinguished first element is characterized internally as the unique
element with no predecessor. This gives a structural description of $1$ that
does not depend on external notation.
depends_on_titles: Distinguished Element Is Not a Successor
Predecessor Existence and Uniqueness Away from $1$

nodes.text_preview: [The Distinguished Element Is the Unique Non-Successor] Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. An element $x P$ is not a successor of any element of $P$ if and only if $x=1$.

nodes.name: No Object Is Its Own Successor
nodes.question: State the theorem: No Object Is Its Own Successor.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $n \in P$,
\[
S(n) \neq n.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $n \in P$,
\[
S(n) \neq n.
\]
nodes.source_text:
Successor always moves forward in the counting structure. No element can be
recovered by applying successor to itself.
depends_on_titles: Distinguished Element Is Not a Successor

nodes.name: Strong Induction on a Peano System
nodes.question: State the theorem: Strong Induction on a Peano System.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $\varphi$ be a predicate on $P$.
If
\[
\forall n \in P\;
\bigl(\forall k \in P\;(k < n \Longrightarrow \varphi(k))\bigr)
\Longrightarrow \varphi(n),
\]
then
\[
\forall n \in P\;\varphi(n).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $\varphi$ be a predicate on $P$.
If
\[
\forall n \in P\;
\bigl(\forall k \in P\;(k < n \Longrightarrow \varphi(k))\bigr)
\Longrightarrow \varphi(n),
\]
then
\[
\forall n \in P\;\varphi(n).
\]
nodes.source_text:
Strong induction grants the inductive step access to the entire history below
$n$, not just the single predecessor. The contrapositive gives the least
counterexample principle: if any element fails $\varphi$, there is a least
one, and that least one has $\varphi$ holding at all its predecessors, which
contradicts the strong-induction hypothesis. This makes strong induction and
least-counterexample arguments two descriptions of the same logical structure.
depends_on_titles: Strict Less Than on a Peano System
Induction Principle for a Peano System

nodes.name: Induction from an Arbitrary Base
nodes.question: State the theorem: Induction from an Arbitrary Base.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $m \in P$, and let $\varphi$ be a
predicate on $P$. If
\[
\varphi(m)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;\bigl(n \ge m \land \varphi(n) \Longrightarrow \varphi(S(n))\bigr),
\]
then
\[
\forall n \in P\;\bigl(n \ge m \Longrightarrow \varphi(n)\bigr).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $m \in P$, and let $\varphi$ be a
predicate on $P$. If
\[
\varphi(m)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;\bigl(n \ge m \land \varphi(n) \Longrightarrow \varphi(S(n))\bigr),
\]
then
\[
\forall n \in P\;\bigl(n \ge m \Longrightarrow \varphi(n)\bigr).
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system, } m \in P,
\text{ and } \varphi \text{ a predicate on } P.\\
&\Bigl(\varphi(m) \land
\forall n \in P\;\bigl(n \ge m \land \varphi(n)
\Longrightarrow \varphi(S(n))\bigr)\Bigr)
\Longrightarrow
\forall n \in P\;\bigl(n \ge m \Longrightarrow \varphi(n)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system, } m \in P,
\text{ and } \varphi \text{ a predicate on } P.\\
&\Bigl(\varphi(m) \land
\forall n \in P\;\bigl(n \ge m \land \varphi(n)
\Longrightarrow \varphi(S(n))\bigr)\Bigr)
\Longrightarrow
\forall n \in P\;\bigl(n \ge m \Longrightarrow \varphi(n)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.source_text:
Apply ordinary induction to the shifted predicate $\psi(n) \coloneqq
(n \ge m \Longrightarrow \varphi(n))$, which holds vacuously below $m$
and reduces to $\varphi$ above $m$. This theorem is the formal license for
all arguments of the form ``for all $n \ge m$, proceed by induction.''
depends_on_titles: Less Than or Equal on a Peano System
Induction Principle for a Peano System

nodes.name: Multiplication on a Peano System
nodes.question: State the definition: Multiplication on a Peano System.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For each $m \in P$, define the function
\[
g_m : P \to P
\]
to be the unique function satisfying
\[
g_m(1)=m
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(g_m(S(n))=g_m(n)+m).
\]
For $m,n \in P$, define
\[
m \cdot n \coloneqq g_m(n).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For each $m \in P$, define the function
\[
g_m : P \to P
\]
to be the unique function satisfying
\[
g_m(1)=m
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(g_m(S(n))=g_m(n)+m).
\]
For $m,n \in P$, define
\[
m \cdot n \coloneqq g_m(n).
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system. For each } m \in P,
\text{ let } g_m : P \to P \text{ satisfy}\\
&g_m(1)=m
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(g_m(S(n))=g_m(n)+m).\\
&\text{Then for all } m,n \in P,\quad m \cdot n \coloneqq g_m(n).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system. For each } m \in P,
\text{ let } g_m : P \to P \text{ satisfy}\\
&g_m(1)=m
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(g_m(S(n))=g_m(n)+m).\\
&\text{Then for all } m,n \in P,\quad m \cdot n \coloneqq g_m(n).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system. For each } m \in P,
\text{ let } g_m : P \to P \text{ satisfy}\\
&g_m(1)=m
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(g_m(S(n))=g_m(n)+m).\\
&\text{Then for all } m,n,x \in P,\quad x \neq m \cdot n
\Longleftrightarrow
x \neq g_m(n).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system. For each } m \in P,
\text{ let } g_m : P \to P \text{ satisfy}\\
&g_m(1)=m
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(g_m(S(n))=g_m(n)+m).\\
&\text{Then for all } m,n,x \in P,\quad x \neq m \cdot n
\Longleftrightarrow
x \neq g_m(n).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.failure_modes:
The definition fails only when a proposed value for $m \cdot n$ does not agree
with the recursively generated function attached to the fixed left factor $m$.
Once $g_m$ is determined, every incorrect candidate product is simply a value
different from $g_m(n)$.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system. For each } m \in P,
\text{ let } g_m : P \to P \text{ satisfy}\\
&g_m(1)=m
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(g_m(S(n))=g_m(n)+m).\\
&x \neq m \cdot n
\Longleftrightarrow
\underbrace{x \neq g_m(n)}_{\text{candidate value disagrees with the recursive output}}.
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.source_text:
Multiplication is introduced by fixing the left input and recursively
generating the right-input map. The value $m \cdot 1$ is initialized at $m$,
and each successor step in the right input adds one further copy of $m$.
Multiplication is therefore repeated addition.

nodes.name: Multiplication Is Well-Defined on a Peano System
nodes.question: State the theorem: Multiplication Is Well-Defined on a Peano System.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m \in P$, there exists a unique
function
\[
g_m : P \to P
\]
such that
\[
g_m(1)=m
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(g_m(S(n))=g_m(n)+m).
\]
Consequently the rule
\[
(m,n) \longmapsto m \cdot n \coloneqq g_m(n)
\]
defines a unique binary operation on $P$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m \in P$, there exists a unique
function
\[
g_m : P \to P
\]
such that
\[
g_m(1)=m
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(g_m(S(n))=g_m(n)+m).
\]
Consequently the rule
\[
(m,n) \longmapsto m \cdot n \coloneqq g_m(n)
\]
defines a unique binary operation on $P$.
nodes.source_text:
The recursive clauses for multiplication determine exactly one right-input map
for each fixed left input. The multiplication operation is therefore
constructed from recursion and addition rather than assumed at the outset.
depends_on_titles: General Recursion Theorem for a Peano System

nodes.name: Multiplication With $1$
nodes.question: State the theorem: Multiplication With $1$.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m \in P$,
\[
m \cdot 1 = m.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m \in P$,
\[
m \cdot 1 = m.
\]
nodes.source_text:
This theorem records the base clause of the recursive definition in operation
notation. Multiplying by the distinguished first element leaves the left input
unchanged.
used_by_titles: $ $ Is a Commutative Monoid Under Multiplication

nodes.name: Multiplication Successor on the Right
nodes.question: State the theorem: Multiplication Successor on the Right.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m,n \in P$,
\[
m \cdot S(n) = (m \cdot n) + m.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m,n \in P$,
\[
m \cdot S(n) = (m \cdot n) + m.
\]
nodes.source_text:
Moving one successor step forward in the right input adds one further copy of
$m$ to the product. This is the recursive step of multiplication written in
operation notation.
nodes.text_preview: [Multiplication Successor on the Right] Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m,n P$, \[ m S(n) = (m n) + m. \]

nodes.name: One Times $n$
nodes.question: State the theorem: One Times $n$.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $n \in P$,
\[
1 \cdot n = n.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $n \in P$,
\[
1 \cdot n = n.
\]
nodes.source_text:
The distinguished first element acts as a multiplicative identity on the left.
This is the multiplicative analogue of the theorem that $1+n$ produces the
successor of $n$ in the additive theory.
depends_on_titles: Induction Principle for a Peano System
used_by_titles: $ $ Is a Commutative Monoid Under Multiplication
nodes.text_preview: [One Times $n$] Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $n P$, \[ 1 n = n. \]

nodes.name: Multiplication Distributes Over Addition
nodes.question: State the theorem: Multiplication Distributes Over Addition.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a \cdot (b+c) = (a \cdot b) + (a \cdot c).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a \cdot (b+c) = (a \cdot b) + (a \cdot c).
\]
nodes.source_text:
Distributivity expresses formally that multiplying by $a$ means repeatedly
adding copies of $a$, so a sum in the second factor splits into two separate
blocks of repeated addition. This is the bridge between the additive and
multiplicative structures.
depends_on_titles: Induction Principle for a Peano System
nodes.text_preview: [Multiplication Distributes Over Addition] Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c P$, \[ a (b+c) = (a b) + (a c). \]

nodes.name: Multiplication Is Associative
nodes.question: State the theorem: Multiplication Is Associative.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
(a \cdot b) \cdot c = a \cdot (b \cdot c).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
(a \cdot b) \cdot c = a \cdot (b \cdot c).
\]
nodes.source_text:
Associativity shows that repeated multiplication can be regrouped without
changing the value. Once this law is established, longer products can be used
without parenthetical ambiguity.
depends_on_titles: Induction Principle for a Peano System
used_by_titles: $ $ Is a Commutative Monoid Under Multiplication
nodes.text_preview: [Multiplication Is Associative] Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c P$, \[ (a b) c = a (b c). \]

nodes.name: Multiplication Is Commutative
nodes.question: State the theorem: Multiplication Is Commutative.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$,
\[
a \cdot b = b \cdot a.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$,
\[
a \cdot b = b \cdot a.
\]
nodes.source_text:
Although multiplication is defined recursively in one argument, the resulting
operation is symmetric in the two factors. Commutativity shows that repeated
addition of $a$ to build $a \cdot b$ agrees exactly with repeated addition of
$b$ to build $b \cdot a$.
depends_on_titles: Induction Principle for a Peano System
used_by_titles: $ $ Is a Commutative Monoid Under Multiplication
nodes.text_preview: [Multiplication Is Commutative] Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b P$, \[ a b = b a. \]

nodes.name: Strict Less Than on a Peano System
nodes.question: State the definition: Strict Less Than on a Peano System.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $a,b \in P$. One writes
\[
a<b
\]
exactly when there exists $c \in P$ such that
\[
b=a+c.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $a,b \in P$. One writes
\[
a<b
\]
exactly when there exists $c \in P$ such that
\[
b=a+c.
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } a,b \in P.\\
&a<b
\Longleftrightarrow
\exists c \in P\;(b=a+c).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } a,b \in P.\\
&a<b
\Longleftrightarrow
\exists c \in P\;(b=a+c).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } a,b \in P.\\
&a \not< b
\Longleftrightarrow
\forall c \in P\;(b \neq a+c).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } a,b \in P.\\
&a \not< b
\Longleftrightarrow
\forall c \in P\;(b \neq a+c).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.failure_modes:
Strict inequality fails exactly when no additive witness in $P$ carries $a$ to
$b$. In that case, every nontrivial additive step from $a$ misses $b$.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } a,b \in P.\\
&a \not< b
\Longleftrightarrow
\underbrace{\forall c \in P\;(b \neq a+c)}_{\text{every proposed additive witness fails}}.
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.source_text:
The strict order records whether $b$ lies genuinely ahead of $a$ in the
counting structure. The witness $c$ is the amount of additive displacement
needed to move from $a$ to $b$.

nodes.name: Less Than or Equal on a Peano System
nodes.question: State the definition: Less Than or Equal on a Peano System.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $a,b \in P$. One writes
\[
a \le b
\]
exactly when
\[
a<b
\qquad\text{or}\qquad
a=b.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $a,b \in P$. One writes
\[
a \le b
\]
exactly when
\[
a<b
\qquad\text{or}\qquad
a=b.
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } a,b \in P.\\
&a \le b
\Longleftrightarrow
\bigl(a<b \lor a=b\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } a,b \in P.\\
&a \le b
\Longleftrightarrow
\bigl(a<b \lor a=b\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } a,b \in P.\\
&a \not\le b
\Longleftrightarrow
\bigl(a \not< b \land a \neq b\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } a,b \in P.\\
&a \not\le b
\Longleftrightarrow
\bigl(a \not< b \land a \neq b\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.failure_modes:
The non-strict order fails in exactly two simultaneous ways: $a$ is not
strictly below $b$, and $a$ is not equal to $b$. Thus $b$ neither lies ahead
of $a$ nor coincides with it.
nodes.source_text:
The relation $a \le b$ allows either equality or a genuine forward step from
$a$ to $b$. It is the additive order relation derived from the strict witness
definition.
depends_on_titles: Strict Less Than on a Peano System

nodes.name: Order Is Reflexive on a Peano System
nodes.question: State the theorem: Order Is Reflexive on a Peano System.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a \in P$,
\[
a \le a.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a \in P$,
\[
a \le a.
\]
nodes.source_text:
Every element is comparable to itself. Reflexivity supplies the equality case
inside the non-strict order.
depends_on_titles: Less Than or Equal on a Peano System

nodes.name: Order Is Transitive on a Peano System
nodes.question: State the theorem: Order Is Transitive on a Peano System.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
\bigl(a \le b \land b \le c\bigr)
\Longrightarrow
a \le c.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
\bigl(a \le b \land b \le c\bigr)
\Longrightarrow
a \le c.
\]
nodes.source_text:
If $b$ lies at or beyond $a$, and $c$ lies at or beyond $b$, then $c$ also
lies at or beyond $a$. Transitivity expresses the consistency of additive
comparison along a chain.
depends_on_titles: Less Than or Equal on a Peano System

nodes.name: Order Is Antisymmetric on a Peano System
nodes.question: State the theorem: Order Is Antisymmetric on a Peano System.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$,
\[
\bigl(a \le b \land b \le a\bigr)
\Longrightarrow
a=b.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$,
\[
\bigl(a \le b \land b \le a\bigr)
\Longrightarrow
a=b.
\]
nodes.source_text:
Two distinct elements cannot each lie at or beyond the other. Antisymmetry
separates genuine equality from bidirectional comparability.
depends_on_titles: Less Than or Equal on a Peano System
Strict Less Than on a Peano System
No Object Is Its Own Successor

nodes.name: Addition Preserves Order on the Right
nodes.question: State the theorem: Addition Preserves Order on the Right.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a \le b
\Longrightarrow
a+c \le b+c.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a \le b
\Longrightarrow
a+c \le b+c.
\]
nodes.source_text:
Adding the same quantity to two comparable elements does not disturb their
order. The additive witness for $a \le b$ survives after both sides are shifted
by the same summand.
depends_on_titles: Less Than or Equal on a Peano System
Strict Less Than on a Peano System

nodes.name: Addition Preserves Order on the Left
nodes.question: State the theorem: Addition Preserves Order on the Left.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a \le b
\Longrightarrow
c+a \le c+b.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a \le b
\Longrightarrow
c+a \le c+b.
\]
nodes.source_text:
The same order preservation holds when the common summand is added on the
left. This is the left-handed form of the additive compatibility law.
depends_on_titles: Addition Preserves Order on the Right

nodes.name: Strict Order Successor Characterization
nodes.question: State the theorem: Strict Order Successor Characterization.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$,
\[
a<b
\Longleftrightarrow
S(a) \le b.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$,
\[
a<b
\Longleftrightarrow
S(a) \le b.
\]
nodes.source_text:
To say that $b$ is strictly larger than $a$ is to say that $b$ lies at or
beyond the first successor step past $a$. This theorem converts strict order
into a non-strict comparison involving successor.
depends_on_titles: Less Than or Equal on a Peano System

nodes.name: Trichotomy on a Peano System
nodes.question: State the theorem: Trichotomy on a Peano System.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$, exactly one of the
following holds:
\[
a<b,\qquad a=b,\qquad b<a.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$, exactly one of the
following holds:
\[
a<b,\qquad a=b,\qquad b<a.
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system.}\\
&\forall a,b \in P\;
\Bigl(
(a<b \lor a=b \lor b<a) \land {}\\
&\qquad\neg(a<b \land a=b) \land
\neg(a<b \land b<a) \land
\neg(a=b \land b<a)
\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system.}\\
&\forall a,b \in P\;
\Bigl(
(a<b \lor a=b \lor b<a) \land {}\\
&\qquad\neg(a<b \land a=b) \land
\neg(a<b \land b<a) \land
\neg(a=b \land b<a)
\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.source_text:
Any two elements of a Peano system are linearly comparable: one lies below the
other, or they coincide. Trichotomy is the theorem that upgrades the additive
comparison relation from a partial-order structure to a total order.

nodes.name: Exponentiation on a Peano System
nodes.question: State the definition: Exponentiation on a Peano System.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For each $a \in P$, define the function
\[
h_a : P \to P
\]
to be the unique function satisfying
\[
h_a(1)=a
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(h_a(S(n))=h_a(n)\cdot a).
\]
For $a,n \in P$, define
\[
a^n \coloneqq h_a(n).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For each $a \in P$, define the function
\[
h_a : P \to P
\]
to be the unique function satisfying
\[
h_a(1)=a
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(h_a(S(n))=h_a(n)\cdot a).
\]
For $a,n \in P$, define
\[
a^n \coloneqq h_a(n).
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system. For each } a \in P,
\text{ let } h_a : P \to P \text{ satisfy}\\
&h_a(1)=a
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(h_a(S(n))=h_a(n)\cdot a).\\
&\text{Then for all } a,n \in P,\quad a^n \coloneqq h_a(n).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system. For each } a \in P,
\text{ let } h_a : P \to P \text{ satisfy}\\
&h_a(1)=a
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(h_a(S(n))=h_a(n)\cdot a).\\
&\text{Then for all } a,n \in P,\quad a^n \coloneqq h_a(n).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system. For each } a \in P,
\text{ let } h_a : P \to P \text{ satisfy}\\
&h_a(1)=a
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(h_a(S(n))=h_a(n)\cdot a).\\
&\text{Then for all } a,n,x \in P,\quad
x \neq a^n \Longleftrightarrow x \neq h_a(n).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system. For each } a \in P,
\text{ let } h_a : P \to P \text{ satisfy}\\
&h_a(1)=a
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(h_a(S(n))=h_a(n)\cdot a).\\
&\text{Then for all } a,n,x \in P,\quad
x \neq a^n \Longleftrightarrow x \neq h_a(n).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.source_text:
Exponentiation is repeated multiplication in the same sense that multiplication
is repeated addition. The base $a$ is the fixed left factor; the exponent $n$
counts how many times $a$ is multiplied together. The recursive definition
propagates rightward: $a^1 = a$, $a^2 = a^1 \cdot a = a \cdot a$, and so on.
depends_on_titles: General Recursion Theorem for a Peano System

nodes.name: Exponent Sum Law
nodes.question: State the theorem: Exponent Sum Law.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For all $a,m,n \in P$,
\[
a^{m+n} = a^m \cdot a^n.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For all $a,m,n \in P$,
\[
a^{m+n} = a^m \cdot a^n.
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system.}\quad
\forall a,m,n \in P\;(a^{m+n} = a^m \cdot a^n).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system.}\quad
\forall a,m,n \in P\;(a^{m+n} = a^m \cdot a^n).
\]
nodes.source_text:
Adding exponents corresponds to concatenating the two blocks of repeated
multiplication. The proof is induction on $n$ using the recursive clause
$a^{S(n)} = a^n \cdot a$ and associativity of multiplication.
depends_on_titles: Induction Principle for a Peano System
nodes.text_preview: [Exponent Sum Law] Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For all $a,m,n P$, \[ a^ m+n = a^m a^n. \]

nodes.name: Exponent Product Law
nodes.question: State the theorem: Exponent Product Law.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For all $a,b,n \in P$,
\[
(a \cdot b)^n = a^n \cdot b^n.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For all $a,b,n \in P$,
\[
(a \cdot b)^n = a^n \cdot b^n.
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system.}\quad
\forall a,b,n \in P\;((a \cdot b)^n = a^n \cdot b^n).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system.}\quad
\forall a,b,n \in P\;((a \cdot b)^n = a^n \cdot b^n).
\]
nodes.source_text:
Raising a product to a power distributes over the factors. The proof is
induction on $n$, using commutativity and associativity of multiplication to
rearrange the interleaved factors at each successor step.
depends_on_titles: Induction Principle for a Peano System
nodes.text_preview: [Exponent Product Law] Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For all $a,b,n P$, \[ (a b)^n = a^n b^n. \]

nodes.name: Exponent Power Law
nodes.question: State the theorem: Exponent Power Law.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For all $a,m,n \in P$,
\[
(a^m)^n = a^{m \cdot n}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For all $a,m,n \in P$,
\[
(a^m)^n = a^{m \cdot n}.
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system.}\quad
\forall a,m,n \in P\;((a^m)^n = a^{m \cdot n}).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system.}\quad
\forall a,m,n \in P\;((a^m)^n = a^{m \cdot n}).
\]
nodes.source_text:
Raising a power to a further power multiplies the exponents. The proof is
induction on $n$ using the exponent sum law and the recursive definition of
multiplication.

nodes.name: $n < 2^n$ for All $n $
nodes.question: State the theorem: $n < 2^n$ for All $n $.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $2 \coloneqq S(1)$. For every $n \in P$,
\[
n < 2^n.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $2 \coloneqq S(1)$. For every $n \in P$,
\[
n < 2^n.
\]
nodes.source_text:
The base case $1 < 2 = 2^1$ holds by definition of $2$ and the strict order.
For the inductive step, assume $n < 2^n$. Then $2^{S(n)} = 2^n \cdot 2$.
Since $2^n > n \ge 1$, we have $2^n \cdot 2 > n \cdot 2 = n + n > n + 1 = S(n)$,
where the last step uses that $n \ge 1$ so $n + n > n + 1$. Hence $S(n) < 2^{S(n)}$.
The inequality $n < 2^n$ is the threshold that distinguishes the growth regimes
and reappears in every comparison between polynomial and exponential bounds.
depends_on_titles: Strict Less Than on a Peano System
Addition Preserves Order on the Right
Induction Principle for a Peano System

nodes.name: Iterator Data on a Peano System
nodes.question: State the definition: Iterator Data on a Peano System.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $X$ be a set. A pair $(a,T)$ is
called \emph{iterator data on $X$} exactly when $a \in X$ and
\[
T : X \to X
\]
is a function.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $X$ be a set. A pair $(a,T)$ is
called \emph{iterator data on $X$} exactly when $a \in X$ and
\[
T : X \to X
\]
is a function.
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } X \text{ be a set.}\\
&(a,T) \text{ is iterator data on } X
\Longleftrightarrow
\bigl(a \in X \land T : X \to X\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } X \text{ be a set.}\\
&(a,T) \text{ is iterator data on } X
\Longleftrightarrow
\bigl(a \in X \land T : X \to X\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } X \text{ be a set.}\\
&(a,T) \text{ is not iterator data on } X
\Longleftrightarrow
\bigl(a \notin X \lor T \text{ is not a function } X \to X\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system and let } X \text{ be a set.}\\
&(a,T) \text{ is not iterator data on } X
\Longleftrightarrow
\bigl(a \notin X \lor T \text{ is not a function } X \to X\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.failure_modes:
Iterator data fails in exactly two ways: the proposed initial value may lie
outside the target set, or the proposed update rule may fail to be a self-map
of that target set.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&(a,T) \text{ is not iterator data on } X\\
\Longleftrightarrow\;&
\underbrace{a \notin X}_{\text{invalid initial value}}\\
&\lor
\underbrace{T \text{ is not a function } X \to X}_{\text{invalid update rule}}.
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.source_text:
Iterator data specifies the target of the recursive construction, the first
value to be assigned, and the update rule that carries one stage to the next.
The Peano system supplies the indexing structure; the pair $(a,T)$ supplies the
data to be propagated along that structure.
nodes.text_preview: [Iterator Data on a Peano System] Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $X$ be a set. A pair $(a,T)$ is called exactly when $a X$ and \[ T : X X \] is a function.

nodes.name: Peano Iterator Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Peano Iterator Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $X$ be a set, and let $(a,T)$ be iterator
data on $X$. Then there exists a unique function
\[
f : P \to X
\]
such that
\[
f(1)=a
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(f(S(n))=T(f(n))).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $X$ be a set, and let $(a,T)$ be iterator
data on $X$. Then there exists a unique function
\[
f : P \to X
\]
such that
\[
f(1)=a
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(f(S(n))=T(f(n))).
\]
nodes.source_text:
The theorem states that a Peano system supports recursive definition by
iteration. Once the first value and the update rule are fixed, exactly one
function is generated. This is the basic mechanism from which recursively
defined arithmetic operations arise.
depends_on_titles: Iterator Data on a Peano System
Induction Principle for a Peano System

nodes.text_preview: [Peano Iterator Theorem] Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $X$ be a set, and let $(a,T)$ be iterator data on $X$. Then there exists a unique function \[ f : P X \] such that \[ f(1)=a n P\;(f(S(n))=T(f(n))). \]

nodes.name: Uniqueness of Recursively Defined Functions
nodes.question: State the corollary: Uniqueness of Recursively Defined Functions.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $X$ be a set, and let $(a,T)$ be iterator
data on $X$. If $f,g : P \to X$ satisfy
\[
f(1)=a,\qquad g(1)=a,
\]
and
\[
\forall n \in P\;(f(S(n))=T(f(n)))
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(g(S(n))=T(g(n))),
\]
then
\[
f=g.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $X$ be a set, and let $(a,T)$ be iterator
data on $X$. If $f,g : P \to X$ satisfy
\[
f(1)=a,\qquad g(1)=a,
\]
and
\[
\forall n \in P\;(f(S(n))=T(f(n)))
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(g(S(n))=T(g(n))),
\]
then
\[
f=g.
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system, let } X \text{ be a set, and let } (a,T) \text{ be iterator data on } X.\\
&\forall f,g : P \to X\;\Bigl(
f(1)=a \land g(1)=a \land
\forall n \in P\;(f(S(n))=T(f(n))) \\
&\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\land
\forall n \in P\;(g(S(n))=T(g(n)))
\Longrightarrow f=g\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system, let } X \text{ be a set, and let } (a,T) \text{ be iterator data on } X.\\
&\forall f,g : P \to X\;\Bigl(
f(1)=a \land g(1)=a \land
\forall n \in P\;(f(S(n))=T(f(n))) \\
&\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\land
\forall n \in P\;(g(S(n))=T(g(n)))
\Longrightarrow f=g\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.source_text:
The recursive specification determines at most one function. Once the first
value and successor rule are fixed, no ambiguity remains in the values of the
resulting map.
depends_on_titles: Iterator Data on a Peano System

nodes.name: Iterator-Generated Function
nodes.question: State the definition: Iterator-Generated Function.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $X$ be a set, and let $(a,T)$ be iterator
data on $X$. The unique function
\[
f : P \to X
\]
satisfying
\[
f(1)=a
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(f(S(n))=T(f(n)))
\]
is called the \emph{iterator-generated function determined by $(a,T)$}.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $X$ be a set, and let $(a,T)$ be iterator
data on $X$. The unique function
\[
f : P \to X
\]
satisfying
\[
f(1)=a
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(f(S(n))=T(f(n)))
\]
is called the \emph{iterator-generated function determined by $(a,T)$}.
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system, let } X \text{ be a set, and let } (a,T) \text{ be iterator data on } X.\\
&f \text{ is the iterator-generated function determined by } (a,T)\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\Bigl(f : P \to X \land f(1)=a \land
\forall n \in P\;(f(S(n))=T(f(n)))\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system, let } X \text{ be a set, and let } (a,T) \text{ be iterator data on } X.\\
&f \text{ is the iterator-generated function determined by } (a,T)\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\Bigl(f : P \to X \land f(1)=a \land
\forall n \in P\;(f(S(n))=T(f(n)))\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system, let } X \text{ be a set, and let } (a,T) \text{ be iterator data on } X.\\
&f \text{ is not the iterator-generated function determined by } (a,T)\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\Bigl(f : P \to X \land
\bigl(f(1)\neq a \lor \exists n \in P\;(f(S(n))\neq T(f(n)))\bigr)\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system, let } X \text{ be a set, and let } (a,T) \text{ be iterator data on } X.\\
&f \text{ is not the iterator-generated function determined by } (a,T)\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\Bigl(f : P \to X \land
\bigl(f(1)\neq a \lor \exists n \in P\;(f(S(n))\neq T(f(n)))\bigr)\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.source_text:
This definition packages the unique output of the iterator theorem as a named
mathematical object. It turns the existence-and-uniqueness theorem into
notation that can be reused in later recursive constructions.

$(a,T)$ is called the \emph{iteration of the self-map $T$ from the initial
value $a$ along $P$}.

value $a$ along $P$}.

nodes.source_text:
Iteration packages recursive generation by repeated application of a single
self-map. This is the canonical recursion pattern behind repeated succession,
repeated addition, and repeated multiplication.

nodes.name: Recursive Function on a Peano System
nodes.question: State the definition: Recursive Function on a Peano System.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $X$ be a set, let $a \in X$, and let
\[
\Phi : P \times X \to X
\]
be a function. A function
\[
f : P \to X
\]
is called \emph{recursive with initial value $a$ and step rule $\Phi$} exactly
when
\[
f(1)=a
\]
and
\[
\forall n \in P\;(f(S(n))=\Phi(n,f(n))).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $X$ be a set, let $a \in X$, and let
\[
\Phi : P \times X \to X
\]
be a function. A function
\[
f : P \to X
\]
is called \emph{recursive with initial value $a$ and step rule $\Phi$} exactly
when
\[
f(1)=a
\]
and
\[
\forall n \in P\;(f(S(n))=\Phi(n,f(n))).
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system, let } X \text{ be a set, let } a \in X, \text{ and let } \Phi : P \times X \to X.\\
&f \text{ is recursive with initial value } a \text{ and step rule } \Phi\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\Bigl(f : P \to X \land f(1)=a \land
\forall n \in P\;(f(S(n))=\Phi(n,f(n)))\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system, let } X \text{ be a set, let } a \in X, \text{ and let } \Phi : P \times X \to X.\\
&f \text{ is recursive with initial value } a \text{ and step rule } \Phi\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\Bigl(f : P \to X \land f(1)=a \land
\forall n \in P\;(f(S(n))=\Phi(n,f(n)))\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system, let } X \text{ be a set, let } a \in X, \text{ and let } \Phi : P \times X \to X.\\
&f \text{ is not recursive with initial value } a \text{ and step rule } \Phi\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\Bigl(f : P \to X \land
\bigl(f(1)\neq a \lor \exists n \in P\;(f(S(n))\neq \Phi(n,f(n)))\bigr)\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system, let } X \text{ be a set, let } a \in X, \text{ and let } \Phi : P \times X \to X.\\
&f \text{ is not recursive with initial value } a \text{ and step rule } \Phi\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\Bigl(f : P \to X \land
\bigl(f(1)\neq a \lor \exists n \in P\;(f(S(n))\neq \Phi(n,f(n)))\bigr)\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.failure_modes:
A function fails to satisfy the recursive specification if the initial value is
wrong or if the successor step fails at some stage of the Peano system.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&f \text{ is not recursive with initial value } a \text{ and step rule } \Phi\\
\Longleftrightarrow\;&
\underbrace{f(1)\neq a}_{\text{base clause fails}}\\
&\lor
\underbrace{\exists n \in P\;(f(S(n))\neq \Phi(n,f(n)))}_{\text{recursive step fails}}.
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.source_text:
This definition allows the recursive step to depend on the current stage as
well as the current value. It generalizes pure iteration and captures the
standard form of recursive definition used throughout arithmetic.

nodes.name: Recursive Specification Is Well-Defined
nodes.question: State the theorem: Recursive Specification Is Well-Defined.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $X$ be a set, let $a \in X$, and let
\[
\Phi : P \times X \to X
\]
be a function. Then there exists at most one recursive function on $P$ with
initial value $a$ and step rule $\Phi$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $X$ be a set, let $a \in X$, and let
\[
\Phi : P \times X \to X
\]
be a function. Then there exists at most one recursive function on $P$ with
initial value $a$ and step rule $\Phi$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system, let } X \text{ be a set, let } a \in X, \text{ and let } \Phi : P \times X \to X.\\
&\forall f,g : P \to X\;\Bigl(
f(1)=a \land g(1)=a \land
\forall n \in P\;(f(S(n))=\Phi(n,f(n))) \\
&\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\land
\forall n \in P\;(g(S(n))=\Phi(n,g(n)))
\Longrightarrow f=g\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } (P,S,1) \text{ be a Peano system, let } X \text{ be a set, let } a \in X, \text{ and let } \Phi : P \times X \to X.\\
&\forall f,g : P \to X\;\Bigl(
f(1)=a \land g(1)=a \land
\forall n \in P\;(f(S(n))=\Phi(n,f(n))) \\
&\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\land
\forall n \in P\;(g(S(n))=\Phi(n,g(n)))
\Longrightarrow f=g\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.source_text:
Once the initial value and stage-dependent step rule are fixed, the recursive
specification cannot determine two different functions. This is the uniqueness
half of general recursion.

nodes.source_text:
General recursion allows the successor step to depend on the current stage as
well as the current value. This theorem is the full recursive construction
principle from which the basic arithmetic operations on the natural numbers are
defined.

nodes.name: Well-Ordering Principle
nodes.question: State the theorem: Well-Ordering Principle.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. Every nonempty subset of $P$ has a least
element with respect to the order $\le$ on $P$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. Every nonempty subset of $P$ has a least
element with respect to the order $\le$ on $P$.
nodes.failure_modes:
The well-ordering principle would fail if some nonempty subset of $P$ had no
least element. That means for every candidate minimum, a strictly smaller
element of the subset exists, producing an infinite strictly descending chain
in $P$. Induction rules this out.
nodes.source_text:
The well-ordering principle is the order-theoretic complement to induction.
Induction says one can build up: start at $1$ and keep stepping forward.
Well-ordering says one can always descend to a minimum: any nonempty set has
a floor. Proof strategies that use well-ordering typically assume a property
fails somewhere, form the set of failures, extract its minimum by
well-ordering, and derive a contradiction from the minimality of that element.
depends_on_titles: Less Than or Equal on a Peano System
Induction Principle for a Peano System
nodes.text_preview: [Well-Ordering Principle] Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. Every nonempty subset of $P$ has a least element with respect to the order $ $ on $P$.

nodes.name: Induction and Well-Ordering Are Equivalent
nodes.question: State the theorem: Induction and Well-Ordering Are Equivalent.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system equipped with the order $\le$. The following
are equivalent:
- The induction principle: for every predicate $\varphi$ on $P$, if
 $\varphi(1)$ holds and the successor step holds, then $\varphi(n)$ holds
 for all $n \in P$.
- The well-ordering principle: every nonempty subset of $P$ has a least
 element with respect to $\le$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system equipped with the order $\le$. The following
are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}
 \item The induction principle: for every predicate $\varphi$ on $P$, if
 $\varphi(1)$ holds and the successor step holds, then $\varphi(n)$ holds
 for all $n \in P$.
 \item The well-ordering principle: every nonempty subset of $P$ has a least
 element with respect to $\le$.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.source_text:
Induction proves well-ordering by contrapositive: if a nonempty set $A$ had
no least element, the set of non-members of $A$ would be inductive, forcing
$A = \varnothing$, a contradiction. Well-ordering proves induction by forming
the set of elements where $\varphi$ fails and, if nonempty, extracting a
least failure to derive a contradiction with the successor step.

nodes.name: Fraction pairs
nodes.question: State the definition: Fraction pairs.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\mathbb{Z}$ denote the set of integers and define
\[
W = \mathbb{Z} \times (\mathbb{Z}\setminus\{0\}).
\]
Thus
\[
W = \{(a,b) : a,b\in\mathbb{Z},\ b\neq 0\}.
\]

Each ordered pair $(a,b)$ is intended to represent the formal fraction $a/b$.
