nodes.name: $ $ Has No Minimum Positive Element
nodes.question: State the theorem: $ $ Has No Minimum Positive Element.
nodes.statement_display:
For every $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $q>0$, there exists $r\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that
\[
0<r<q.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
For every $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $q>0$, there exists $r\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that
\[
0<r<q.
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall q\in\mathbb{Q}\;
\bigl(q>0 \Longrightarrow \exists r\in\mathbb{Q}\;(0<r<q)\bigr).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall q\in\mathbb{Q}\;
\bigl(q>0 \Longrightarrow \exists r\in\mathbb{Q}\;(0<r<q)\bigr).
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\exists q\in\mathbb{Q}\;
\bigl(q>0 \land \forall r\in\mathbb{Q}\;(0<r \Longrightarrow q\le r)\bigr).
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\exists q\in\mathbb{Q}\;
\bigl(q>0 \land \forall r\in\mathbb{Q}\;(0<r \Longrightarrow q\le r)\bigr).
\]
nodes.failure_modes:
This would fail only if some positive rational were the smallest positive
rational. Halving it produces a strictly smaller positive rational, so no such
minimum can exist.
nodes.proof_sketch:
\textbf{Professional Standard Proof.}~\\
Let $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ with
\[
q>0.
\]
Set
\[
r:=\frac{q}{2}.
\]
Since $\mathbb{Q}$ is closed under division by the nonzero rational number
$2$, we have
\[
r\in\mathbb{Q}.
\]

Because $q>0$ and $2>0$, division by the positive rational number $2$
preserves positivity.  Hence
\[
0<\frac{q}{2}=r.
\]

Also, multiplying the inequality $0<q$ by the positive rational number
$\frac{1}{2}$ gives
\[
0<\frac{q}{2}<q.
\]
Thus
\[
0<r<q.
\]

Therefore there exists a rational number $r$ such that
\[
0<r<q,
\]
as required.
nodes.proof_idea:
The proof uses the simplest possible perturbation: halve the given positive
rational.  The number $q/2$ remains rational by closure of $\mathbb{Q}$ under
division by a nonzero rational, and it lies strictly between $0$ and $q$
because division by the positive rational number $2$ preserves order.  So no
positive rational can be smallest: one can always move halfway toward $0$.
nodes.source_text:
Positive rationals can always be halved. There is no smallest positive
rational number.
depends_on_titles: Multiplication by a Positive Element Preserves Order
nodes.text_preview: [$ $ Has No Minimum Positive Element] For every $q $ with $q>0$, there exists $r $ such that \[ 0<r<q. \]

nodes.name: Upper/Lower Perturbation Lemma, Rational Version
nodes.question: State the lemma: Upper/Lower Perturbation Lemma, Rational Version.
nodes.statement_display:
If $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ and $\varepsilon\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $\varepsilon>0$,
then there exist $r,s\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that
\[
q-\varepsilon < r < q < s < q+\varepsilon.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
If $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ and $\varepsilon\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $\varepsilon>0$,
then there exist $r,s\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that
\[
q-\varepsilon < r < q < s < q+\varepsilon.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
\textbf{Professional Standard Proof.}~\\
Let $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ and let $\varepsilon\in\mathbb{Q}$ with
\[
\varepsilon>0.
\]
By
\hyperref[thm:q-has-no-minimum-positive-element]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:q-has-no-minimum-positive-element}},
there exists $\delta\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that
\[
0<\delta<\varepsilon.
\]
Define
\[
r:=q-\delta
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
s:=q+\delta.
\]
Since $\mathbb{Q}$ is closed under addition and subtraction, we have
\[
r,s\in\mathbb{Q}.
\]

Because $\delta>0$, we have
\[
q-\delta<q<q+\delta,
\]
that is,
\[
r<q<s.
\]
And because $\delta<\varepsilon$, we have
\[
q-\varepsilon<q-\delta=r
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
s=q+\delta<q+\varepsilon.
\]
Combining these inequalities yields
\[
q-\varepsilon<r<q<s<q+\varepsilon.
\]
Thus there exist rational numbers $r,s$ with the required properties.
nodes.proof_idea:
Choose a smaller positive rational $\delta$ inside the interval $(0,\varepsilon)$.
Then place one rational point $\delta$ below $q$ and one rational point
$\delta$ above $q$.  Since $\delta<\varepsilon$, both perturbed points stay
within the larger window $(q-\varepsilon,q+\varepsilon)$, and since
$\delta>0$, they lie strictly on opposite sides of $q$.
nodes.source_text:
Every rational point has rational points strictly on both sides within any
prescribed positive distance. This is the working tool for punctured-ball
arguments in $\mathbb{Q}$.
nodes.text_preview: [Upper/Lower Perturbation Lemma, Rational Version] If $q $ and $ $ with $ >0$, then there exist $r,s $ such that \[ q- < r < q < s < q+ . \]

nodes.name: Rational Perturbation Lemma
nodes.question: State the lemma: Rational Perturbation Lemma.
nodes.statement_display:
If $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ and $\varepsilon>0$, then there exists $r\in\mathbb{Q}$
such that
\[
0<|r-q|<\varepsilon.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
If $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ and $\varepsilon>0$, then there exists $r\in\mathbb{Q}$
such that
\[
0<|r-q|<\varepsilon.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
Let $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ and let $\varepsilon>0$. By
\hyperref[lem:rational-upper-lower-perturbation]{Lemma~\ref*{lem:rational-upper-lower-perturbation}},
there exist rational numbers $r,s\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that
\[
q-\varepsilon<r<q<s<q+\varepsilon.
\]
We may use the point $r$. Since $r<q$, we have
\[
0<q-r<\varepsilon.
\]
Because $r-q<0$, the definition of absolute value gives
\[
|r-q|=q-r.
\]
Therefore
\[
0<|r-q|<\varepsilon.
\]
Since $r\in\mathbb{Q}$, this proves the claim.
nodes.proof_idea:
The upper/lower perturbation lemma already gives rational points strictly on
both sides of $q$ within distance $\varepsilon$. To obtain the present result,
it is enough to choose one of those points, say $r<q$, and rewrite the
inequality
\[
q-\varepsilon<r<q
\]
as
\[
0<q-r<\varepsilon.
\]
Since $r-q<0$, one has $|r-q|=q-r$, which yields the desired estimate.
nodes.source_text:
Every rational point is a limit point of $\mathbb{Q}$: it can be approached
arbitrarily closely by other rational numbers.

nodes.question: State the theorem: Rational Chain with Uniformly Small Steps.
nodes.statement_display: Let $r,s\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $r0$. Then there exist $m\in\mathbb{N}$ and rational numbers $q_1,q_2,\dots,q_m$ such that \[ q_1=r,\qquad q_m=s, \] and \[ 0
nodes.statement_tex: Let $r,s\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $r0$. Then there exist $m\in\mathbb{N}$ and rational numbers $q_1,q_2,\dots,q_m$ such that \[ q_1=r,\qquad q_m=s, \] and \[ 0
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall r,s,p\in\mathbb{Q}\;
\Bigl(
r<s \land p>0
\Longrightarrow
\exists m\in\mathbb{N}\;\exists q_1,\dots,q_m\in\mathbb{Q}\;
\bigl(q_1=r \land q_m=s \land
\forall k\in\{2,\dots,m\}\;(0<q_k-q_{k-1}<p)\bigr)
\Bigr).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall r,s,p\in\mathbb{Q}\;
\Bigl(
r<s \land p>0
\Longrightarrow
\exists m\in\mathbb{N}\;\exists q_1,\dots,q_m\in\mathbb{Q}\;
\bigl(q_1=r \land q_m=s \land
\forall k\in\{2,\dots,m\}\;(0<q_k-q_{k-1}<p)\bigr)
\Bigr).
\]
nodes.source_text:
Any rational interval can be traversed by finitely many rational steps each
smaller than a prescribed positive bound. This is the discrete counterpart of
the Archimedean property applied to step traversal.

nodes.name: Denominator growth on infinite Stern--Brocot paths
nodes.question: State the lemma: Denominator growth on infinite Stern--Brocot paths.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $a_0/b_0, a_1/b_1, a_2/b_2, \ldots$ be the sequence of endpoint
fractions encountered along any infinite path in the Stern--Brocot tree.
Then the denominators are strictly increasing:
\[
b_0 < b_1 < b_2 < \cdots,
\]
and in particular $b_n \to \infty$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $a_0/b_0, a_1/b_1, a_2/b_2, \ldots$ be the sequence of endpoint
fractions encountered along any infinite path in the Stern--Brocot tree.
Then the denominators are strictly increasing:
\[
b_0 < b_1 < b_2 < \cdots,
\]
and in particular $b_n \to \infty$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall n \in \mathbb{N},\quad b_n < b_{n+1}.
\qquad
\forall M > 0\;\; \exists N \in \mathbb{N}\;\; \forall n \geq N,\quad b_n > M.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall n \in \mathbb{N},\quad b_n < b_{n+1}.
\qquad
\forall M > 0\;\; \exists N \in \mathbb{N}\;\; \forall n \geq N,\quad b_n > M.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
At each step, the path occupies an interval $[a_n/b_n, c_n/d_n]$ where
$b_n c_n - a_n d_n = 1$ by the Farey Neighbor Theorem.  The mediant
inserted at step $n+1$ has denominator $b_n + d_n$.  One endpoint of the
new interval is this mediant; the other is whichever of $a_n/b_n$ or
$c_n/d_n$ was not replaced.

In either case, the new boundary denominator is $b_n + d_n$, which
strictly exceeds both $b_n$ and $d_n$ since $b_n, d_n \geq 1$.  By
induction, the denominators along the path are strictly increasing.
Since they are positive integers and strictly increasing, they are
unbounded: $b_n \to \infty$.
nodes.proof_idea:
Induction on depth, with the key computation $b_n + d_n > b_n$ requiring
only that $d_n \geq 1$.  The divergence to infinity is immediate from
strict monotonicity of a positive-integer sequence.
nodes.source_text:
At each step in the tree the new fraction produced by the mediant has
denominator equal to the sum of the two parent denominators, which
strictly exceeds each of them.  A path that turns left or right at every
level therefore visits fractions with strictly increasing denominators,
and since the denominators are positive integers they must diverge to
infinity.  This is the key lemma that makes the limit theory work: it
guarantees that the Stern--Brocot intervals shrink to zero length.

nodes.name: Stern--Brocot interval lengths shrink to zero
nodes.question: State the lemma: Stern--Brocot interval lengths shrink to zero.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(I_n)_{n \geq 0}$ be the nested rational intervals generated by
the Stern--Brocot refinement process targeting a real number $x > 0$,
where $I_n = [a_n/b_n,\; c_n/d_n]$. Then the interval lengths
$\ell_n = c_n/d_n - a_n/b_n$ satisfy
\[
\lim_{n \to \infty} \ell_n = 0.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(I_n)_{n \geq 0}$ be the nested rational intervals generated by
the Stern--Brocot refinement process targeting a real number $x > 0$,
where $I_n = [a_n/b_n,\; c_n/d_n]$. Then the interval lengths
$\ell_n = c_n/d_n - a_n/b_n$ satisfy
\[
\lim_{n \to \infty} \ell_n = 0.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
At every level the two boundary fractions are Farey neighbors (the
determinant condition $b_n d_n' - a_n c_n' = 1$ is preserved by mediant
insertion, as established in the proof of
\hyperref[lem:sb-denom-growth]{Lemma~\ref*{lem:sb-denom-growth}}).
By the \hyperref[thm:farey-gap]{gap between Farey neighbors},
\[
\ell_n = \frac{c_n}{d_n} - \frac{a_n}{b_n} = \frac{1}{b_n d_n}.
\]
By \hyperref[lem:sb-denom-growth]{Lemma~\ref*{lem:sb-denom-growth}},
$b_n \to \infty$ and $d_n \to \infty$.  Therefore $1/(b_n d_n) \to 0$,
i.e.\ $\ell_n \to 0$.
nodes.proof_idea:
The proof chains two results: the Farey gap formula gives the exact
expression $\ell_n = 1/(b_n d_n)$, and the Denominator Growth Lemma
gives $b_n, d_n \to \infty$.  The Archimedean property converts the
asymptotic statement into a concrete $N$.
nodes.source_text:
By the Farey gap formula, $\ell_n = 1/(b_n d_n)$.
By the Denominator Growth Lemma, both $b_n$ and $d_n$ tend to infinity.
Therefore $1/(b_n d_n) \to 0$.  The Archimedean property of $\mathbb{Q}$
converts this into an explicit $N$: given $\varepsilon > 0$, find $N$
such that $b_N d_N > 1/\varepsilon$.
depends_on_titles: Denominator growth on infinite Stern--Brocot paths

nodes.name: Nested Stern--Brocot intervals contain a unique limit point
nodes.question: State the proposition: Nested Stern--Brocot intervals contain a unique limit point.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(I_n)_{n \geq 0}$ be the nested sequence of closed rational intervals
generated by the Stern--Brocot refinement process targeting $x > 0$,
with $I_n = [a_n/b_n,\; c_n/d_n]$. Then there exists a unique real
number $\xi \in \bigcap_{n=0}^{\infty} I_n$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(I_n)_{n \geq 0}$ be the nested sequence of closed rational intervals
generated by the Stern--Brocot refinement process targeting $x > 0$,
with $I_n = [a_n/b_n,\; c_n/d_n]$. Then there exists a unique real
number $\xi \in \bigcap_{n=0}^{\infty} I_n$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\exists!\, \xi \in \mathbb{R}\;\;
\forall n \in \mathbb{N},\quad
\frac{a_n}{b_n} \leq \xi \leq \frac{c_n}{d_n}.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\exists!\, \xi \in \mathbb{R}\;\;
\forall n \in \mathbb{N},\quad
\frac{a_n}{b_n} \leq \xi \leq \frac{c_n}{d_n}.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
\textbf{Existence.} The sequence $(a_n/b_n)$ is monotone increasing and
bounded above by $c_0/d_0$; the sequence $(c_n/d_n)$ is monotone decreasing
and bounded below by $a_0/b_0$.  By the Monotone Convergence Theorem in
$\mathbb{R}$, both sequences converge:
\[
\alpha = \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{a_n}{b_n} \in \mathbb{R},
\qquad
\beta = \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{c_n}{d_n} \in \mathbb{R}.
\]
Since $a_n/b_n \leq c_n/d_n$ for all $n$, we have $\alpha \leq \beta$.
But
\[
0 \leq \beta - \alpha \leq \ell_n = \frac{1}{b_n d_n}
\]
for all $n$, and $\ell_n \to 0$
by \hyperref[lem:sb-length-zero]{Lemma~\ref*{lem:sb-length-zero}}.
Therefore $\alpha = \beta$.  Set $\xi = \alpha = \beta$; then
$a_n/b_n \leq \xi \leq c_n/d_n$ for all $n$, so $\xi \in \bigcap I_n$.

\textbf{Uniqueness.} If $\xi, \xi' \in \bigcap I_n$, then for every $n$:
\[
|\xi - \xi'| \leq \ell_n \to 0.
\]
Therefore $\xi = \xi'$.
nodes.proof_idea:
A nested intervals argument in $\mathbb{R}$: monotone bounded sequences
converge, the two limits must coincide because the gaps shrink to zero,
and uniqueness is a direct squeeze.  The completeness of $\mathbb{R}$
(via the Monotone Convergence Theorem) is essential — this argument
fails in $\mathbb{Q}$ because $\xi$ need not be rational.
nodes.source_text:
The left endpoints $(a_n/b_n)$ form a monotone increasing sequence bounded
above; the right endpoints $(c_n/d_n)$ form a monotone decreasing sequence
bounded below.  Both converge in $\mathbb{R}$ by the Monotone Convergence
Theorem, and since the interval lengths tend to zero, the two limits must
coincide.  Uniqueness follows immediately: any two points in
$\bigcap I_n$ differ by at most $\ell_n$ for every $n$, hence by zero.

This is where completeness of $\mathbb{R}$ enters: the Monotone Convergence
Theorem requires the least-upper-bound property, which $\mathbb{Q}$ does
not have.  This is precisely why $\xi$ need not be rational.

nodes.name: Every infinite Stern--Brocot path converges to its target
nodes.question: State the corollary: Every infinite Stern--Brocot path converges to its target.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $x > 0$ be a real number and let $(I_n)$ be the Stern--Brocot
refinement sequence targeting $x$. The sequences of left and right
endpoints both converge to $x$:
\[
\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{a_n}{b_n}
= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{c_n}{d_n}
= x.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $x > 0$ be a real number and let $(I_n)$ be the Stern--Brocot
refinement sequence targeting $x$. The sequences of left and right
endpoints both converge to $x$:
\[
\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{a_n}{b_n}
= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{c_n}{d_n}
= x.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
By \hyperref[prop:sb-unique-point]{Proposition~\ref*{prop:sb-unique-point}},
there is a unique $\xi \in \bigcap I_n$ and both endpoint sequences
converge to $\xi$.  By the construction rule, $x \in I_n$ for every $n$
(at each step, the endpoint on the same side as the mediant relative to
$x$ is replaced, keeping $x$ in the new interval).  Therefore
$x \in \bigcap I_n$.  By uniqueness, $\xi = x$.  Hence both endpoint
sequences converge to $x$.
nodes.proof_idea:
A one-step corollary: the unique point in $\bigcap I_n$ is $\xi$; the
target $x$ lies in every $I_n$ by the construction rule; uniqueness forces
$\xi = x$.  All the work is done by the Unique Point Proposition.
nodes.source_text:
By construction, $x \in I_n$ for every $n$: the refinement rule always
replaces the endpoint that would push $x$ outside the interval, so $x$
is retained in $\bigcap I_n$.  But the proposition says this intersection
contains exactly one point, namely $\xi$.  Therefore $\xi = x$, and the
endpoint sequences converge to $x$.
depends_on_titles: Nested Stern--Brocot intervals contain a unique limit point

nodes.name: Infinite Stern--Brocot paths converge to irrationals
nodes.question: State the corollary: Infinite Stern--Brocot paths converge to irrationals.
nodes.statement_display:
A positive real number $x$ corresponds to a \emph{finite} path in the
Stern--Brocot tree if and only if $x$ is rational. Equivalently, $x$
corresponds to an \emph{infinite} path if and only if $x$ is irrational.
nodes.statement_tex:
A positive real number $x$ corresponds to a \emph{finite} path in the
Stern--Brocot tree if and only if $x$ is rational. Equivalently, $x$
corresponds to an \emph{infinite} path if and only if $x$ is irrational.
nodes.proof_sketch:
\textbf{($\Leftarrow$) Finite path $\Rightarrow$ rational.}
If the process terminates at step $N$, then $x$ equals the mediant
$(a_N + c_N)/(b_N + d_N)$, which is a rational number.

\textbf{($\Rightarrow$) Rational $\Rightarrow$ finite path.}
Let $x = p/q \in \mathbb{Q}_{>0}$ in reduced form.  Suppose for
contradiction that the process never terminates, so $x \neq$ mediant at
every step.  Then $x$ lies strictly between $a_n/b_n$ and $c_n/d_n$ for
all $n$, and by the determinant condition $b_n c_n - a_n d_n = 1$, any
fraction strictly between $a_n/b_n$ and $c_n/d_n$ must have denominator
at least $b_n + d_n > b_n$.  Since $p/q$ lies strictly between these
fractions, $q > b_n$ for all $n$.  But by
\hyperref[lem:sb-denom-growth]{Lemma~\ref*{lem:sb-denom-growth}},
$b_n \to \infty$, so $b_n > q$ for all sufficiently large $n$ —
contradicting $q > b_n$.  Therefore the process must terminate.
nodes.proof_idea:
A biconditional proof.  The ($\Leftarrow$) direction is trivial: a mediant
is visibly rational.  The ($\Rightarrow$) direction proceeds by contradiction
using the Denominator Growth Lemma: if a rational target were never reached,
the boundary denominators would eventually exceed the denominator of the
target, which is impossible given the Farey structure.
nodes.source_text:
The refinement process terminates at the first step where $x$ equals the
mediant.  Since every mediant is rational, termination forces $x \in \mathbb{Q}$.
Conversely, if $x = p/q$ is rational, the Denominator Growth Lemma forces
the denominators past $q$ in finitely many steps, at which point $x$ must
have already been identified as a mediant.  So the rational numbers are
exactly those reached by finite paths, and the irrationals are exactly those
requiring infinite paths.  This is the cleanest formulation of the
incompleteness of $\mathbb{Q}$ via the Stern--Brocot tree.
depends_on_titles: Denominator growth on infinite Stern--Brocot paths

nodes.name: Convergence of a rational sequence
nodes.question: State the definition: Convergence of a rational sequence.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a sequence of rational numbers and let $a \in \mathbb{Q}$.
The sequence $(x_n)$ \emph{converges to $a$} if
\[
\forall \varepsilon \in \mathbb{Q},\; \varepsilon > 0 \;\;
\exists N \in \mathbb{N} \;\;
\forall n \geq N, \quad |x_n - a| < \varepsilon.
\]
We write $\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n = a$, or $x_n \to a$ as $n \to \infty$.
The rational number $a$ is called the \emph{limit} of $(x_n)$.
A sequence that does not converge to any element of $\mathbb{Q}$ is
called \emph{divergent in $\mathbb{Q}$}.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a sequence of rational numbers and let $a \in \mathbb{Q}$.
The sequence $(x_n)$ \emph{converges to $a$} if
\[
\forall \varepsilon \in \mathbb{Q},\; \varepsilon > 0 \;\;
\exists N \in \mathbb{N} \;\;
\forall n \geq N, \quad |x_n - a| < \varepsilon.
\]
We write $\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n = a$, or $x_n \to a$ as $n \to \infty$.
The rational number $a$ is called the \emph{limit} of $(x_n)$.
A sequence that does not converge to any element of $\mathbb{Q}$ is
called \emph{divergent in $\mathbb{Q}$}.
nodes.formal:
\[
\operatorname{ConvergesTo}(x_n, a)
\;\coloneqq\;
\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\; \exists N \in \mathbb{N} \;\; \forall n \geq N,
\quad |x_n - a| < \varepsilon.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\operatorname{ConvergesTo}(x_n, a)
\;\coloneqq\;
\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\; \exists N \in \mathbb{N} \;\; \forall n \geq N,
\quad |x_n - a| < \varepsilon.
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\neg\operatorname{ConvergesTo}(x_n, a)
\;\coloneqq\;
\exists \varepsilon > 0 \;\; \forall N \in \mathbb{N} \;\; \exists n \geq N,
\quad |x_n - a| \geq \varepsilon.
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\neg\operatorname{ConvergesTo}(x_n, a)
\;\coloneqq\;
\exists \varepsilon > 0 \;\; \forall N \in \mathbb{N} \;\; \exists n \geq N,
\quad |x_n - a| \geq \varepsilon.
\]
nodes.failure_modes:
A sequence fails to converge to $a$ in one of two ways:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Oscillation:} terms return repeatedly to both sides of $a$
        with spacing bounded away from zero. Example: $((-1)^n)$.
  \item \textbf{Unbounded drift:} terms recede without bound and approach
        no rational number at all. Example: $(n)$.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.source_text:
Think of $\varepsilon$ as a challenge radius: the definition demands that the
sequence eventually stays inside every $\varepsilon$-ball around $a$, no
matter how small the radius. A sequence converges when it can meet every
challenge, and diverges as soon as some challenge cannot be met.

nodes.name: Limits of rational sequences are unique
nodes.question: State the theorem: Limits of rational sequences are unique.
nodes.statement_display: If a sequence of rational numbers converges, then its limit is unique.
nodes.statement_tex: If a sequence of rational numbers converges, then its limit is unique.
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall (x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{Q},\;
\bigl(
\operatorname{ConvergesTo}(x_n, a) \land \operatorname{ConvergesTo}(x_n, b)
\bigr)
\Longrightarrow a = b.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall (x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{Q},\;
\bigl(
\operatorname{ConvergesTo}(x_n, a) \land \operatorname{ConvergesTo}(x_n, b)
\bigr)
\Longrightarrow a = b.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
Suppose $\lim_{n\to\infty} x_n = a$ and $\lim_{n\to\infty} x_n = b$.
Let $\varepsilon > 0$.
By convergence to $a$, there exists $N_1 \in \mathbb{N}$ such that
$|x_n - a| < \varepsilon/2$ for all $n \geq N_1$.
By convergence to $b$, there exists $N_2 \in \mathbb{N}$ such that
$|x_n - b| < \varepsilon/2$ for all $n \geq N_2$.
Set $N = \max\{N_1, N_2\}$ and choose any $n \geq N$.  By the triangle inequality,
\[
|a - b|
= |a - x_n + x_n - b|
\leq |a - x_n| + |x_n - b|
< \frac{\varepsilon}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon}{2}
= \varepsilon.
\]
Since $\varepsilon > 0$ was arbitrary and $|a - b| < \varepsilon$ for all
$\varepsilon > 0$, we conclude $|a - b| = 0$, hence $a = b$.
nodes.proof_idea:
This is the standard $2\varepsilon$ squeeze argument: make both approximation
errors small by halving $\varepsilon$, then combine via the triangle inequality.
The key move is choosing $N$ large enough to activate both convergence witnesses
simultaneously.
nodes.source_text:
A sequence cannot approach two distinct rational numbers simultaneously.
If it did, the two alleged limits would need to be both arbitrarily close to
the same terms, forcing them to be arbitrarily close to each other --- hence
equal. This is the $2\varepsilon$ triangle-inequality squeeze.

proof_file_blocks.title: Limits of rational sequences are unique

nodes.text_preview: [Limits of rational sequences are unique] If a sequence of rational numbers converges, then its limit is unique.

nodes.name: Subsequence of a rational sequence
nodes.question: State the definition: Subsequence of a rational sequence.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a sequence of rational numbers and let $(k_n)$ be a strictly
increasing sequence of natural numbers:
$k_1 < k_2 < k_3 < \cdots$.
The sequence $(x_{k_n})$ is called a \emph{subsequence} of $(x_n)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a sequence of rational numbers and let $(k_n)$ be a strictly
increasing sequence of natural numbers:
$k_1 < k_2 < k_3 < \cdots$.
The sequence $(x_{k_n})$ is called a \emph{subsequence} of $(x_n)$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\bigl(\forall n \in \mathbb{N},\; k_{n+1} > k_n\bigr)
\;\Longrightarrow\;
(x_{k_n}) \text{ is a subsequence of } (x_n).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\bigl(\forall n \in \mathbb{N},\; k_{n+1} > k_n\bigr)
\;\Longrightarrow\;
(x_{k_n}) \text{ is a subsequence of } (x_n).
\]
nodes.source_text:
A subsequence selects an infinite subcollection of terms in their original
order. The key elementary fact is that $k_n \geq n$ for all $n$, so
subsequence indices grow at least as fast as $n$ itself.

nodes.name: Subsequences of convergent sequences converge to the same limit
nodes.question: State the theorem: Subsequences of convergent sequences converge to the same limit.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a sequence of rational numbers with $x_n \to a$.
Then every subsequence $(x_{k_n})$ of $(x_n)$ also converges to $a$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a sequence of rational numbers with $x_n \to a$.
Then every subsequence $(x_{k_n})$ of $(x_n)$ also converges to $a$.
nodes.proof_sketch:
Let $(x_{k_n})$ be any subsequence of $(x_n)$.  Let $\varepsilon > 0$.
Since $x_n \to a$, there exists $N \in \mathbb{N}$ such that
$|x_n - a| < \varepsilon$ for all $n \geq N$.
We claim $k_n \geq n$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$.
Indeed, $k_1 \geq 1$ since $(k_n) \subseteq \mathbb{N}$, and if
$k_n \geq n$ then $k_{n+1} > k_n \geq n$, so $k_{n+1} \geq n+1$.
By induction, $k_n \geq n$ for all $n$.
Therefore, for all $n \geq N$ we have $k_n \geq n \geq N$, hence
$|x_{k_n} - a| < \varepsilon$.
Since $\varepsilon > 0$ was arbitrary, $x_{k_n} \to a$.
nodes.proof_idea:
The structural key is the lemma $k_n \geq n$: it ensures that every
subsequence index eventually exceeds any given threshold $N$, allowing
us to transfer the convergence bound from $(x_n)$ to $(x_{k_n})$.
The induction is the only non-trivial step; everything else is definition-unwinding.
nodes.source_text:
The contrapositive is the working tool: to prove $(x_n)$ diverges it suffices
to find two subsequences converging to distinct limits, or one subsequence
that diverges.

nodes.name: Bounded sequence of rational numbers
nodes.question: State the definition: Bounded sequence of rational numbers.
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\neg\operatorname{Bounded}(x_n)
\;\coloneqq\;
\forall \Gamma > 0 \;\; \exists n \in \mathbb{N},\;\; |x_n| > \Gamma.
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\neg\operatorname{Bounded}(x_n)
\;\coloneqq\;
\forall \Gamma > 0 \;\; \exists n \in \mathbb{N},\;\; |x_n| > \Gamma.
\]
nodes.source_text:
Boundedness is a global envelope condition: one single rational bound must
contain every term.
nodes.text_preview: [Bounded sequence of rational numbers] A sequence $(x_n)$ of rational numbers is: if $ \; n ,\; x_n $; if $ \; n ,\; x_n $; if $ ,\; >0\;\; n ,\; |x_n| $.

nodes.name: Convergent sequences are bounded
nodes.question: State the theorem: Convergent sequences are bounded.
nodes.statement_display: Every convergent sequence of rational numbers is bounded.
nodes.statement_tex: Every convergent sequence of rational numbers is bounded.
nodes.formal:
\[
\operatorname{ConvergesTo}(x_n, a)
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\operatorname{Bounded}(x_n).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\operatorname{ConvergesTo}(x_n, a)
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\operatorname{Bounded}(x_n).
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
Let $(x_n)$ be a sequence of rational numbers with $x_n \to a \in \mathbb{Q}$.
Apply the definition of convergence with $\varepsilon = 1$: there exists
$N \in \mathbb{N}$ such that $|x_n - a| < 1$ for all $n \geq N$.
By the triangle inequality, $|x_n| \leq |x_n - a| + |a| < 1 + |a|$
for all $n \geq N$.
Define
\[
\Gamma = \max\{|x_1|, |x_2|, \ldots, |x_N|, 1 + |a|\}.
\]
Then $|x_n| \leq \Gamma$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, so $(x_n)$ is bounded.
nodes.proof_idea:
The proof splits the sequence into two parts: a finite initial segment
(handled by taking a maximum of finitely many values) and a tail
(controlled by convergence with $\varepsilon = 1$).  The choice
$\varepsilon = 1$ is not special — any positive $\varepsilon$ works — but
$1$ keeps the arithmetic clean.
nodes.source_text:
Convergence forces the tail of the sequence into a bounded neighborhood of
the limit; the finite initial segment is bounded by the maximum of finitely
many terms. The converse fails: $((-1)^n)$ is bounded but does not converge.
nodes.text_preview: [Convergent sequences are bounded] Every convergent sequence of rational numbers is bounded.

nodes.name: Cauchy sequences are bounded
nodes.question: State the theorem: Cauchy sequences are bounded.
nodes.statement_display: Every Cauchy sequence of rational numbers is bounded.
nodes.statement_tex: Every Cauchy sequence of rational numbers is bounded.
nodes.formal:
\[
\operatorname{CauchySequence}(x_n)
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\operatorname{Bounded}(x_n).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\operatorname{CauchySequence}(x_n)
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\operatorname{Bounded}(x_n).
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy sequence of rational numbers.
Apply the Cauchy condition with $\varepsilon = 1$: there exists
$N \in \mathbb{N}$ such that $|x_m - x_n| < 1$ for all $m, n \geq N$.
Taking $n = N + 1$, we get $|x_m - x_{N+1}| < 1$ for all $m \geq N$,
so $|x_m| \leq |x_m - x_{N+1}| + |x_{N+1}| < 1 + |x_{N+1}|$
for all $m \geq N$.
Define
\[
\Gamma = \max\{|x_1|, \ldots, |x_N|, 1 + |x_{N+1}|\}.
\]
Then $|x_n| \leq \Gamma$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$.
nodes.proof_idea:
The architecture is identical to the proof that convergent sequences are bounded,
with one substitution: instead of anchoring the tail to the limit $a$, we anchor
it to the single term $x_{N+1}$.  The Cauchy condition controls
$|x_m - x_{N+1}|$ for all $m \geq N$ in exactly the same way that convergence
controlled $|x_m - a|$.  This structural parallel is not a coincidence: the
Cauchy condition is convergence without a named limit.
nodes.source_text:
The Cauchy condition forces the tail --- all terms beyond index $N$ --- to
lie within distance $1$ of $x_{N+1}$. The finite initial segment contributes
finitely many terms bounded by their maximum absolute value.
used_by_titles: Cauchy sequences are closed under addition and multiplication
Cauchy equivalence is preserved under addition and multiplication
nodes.text_preview: [Cauchy sequences are bounded] Every Cauchy sequence of rational numbers is bounded.

nodes.name: Cauchy sequences are closed under addition and multiplication
nodes.question: State the theorem: Cauchy sequences are closed under addition and multiplication.
nodes.statement_display:
If $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ are Cauchy sequences of rational numbers, then so
are $(x_n + y_n)$ and $(x_n y_n)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
If $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ are Cauchy sequences of rational numbers, then so
are $(x_n + y_n)$ and $(x_n y_n)$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\operatorname{CauchySequence}(x_n) \land \operatorname{CauchySequence}(y_n)
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\operatorname{CauchySequence}(x_n + y_n)
\;\land\;
\operatorname{CauchySequence}(x_n y_n).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\operatorname{CauchySequence}(x_n) \land \operatorname{CauchySequence}(y_n)
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\operatorname{CauchySequence}(x_n + y_n)
\;\land\;
\operatorname{CauchySequence}(x_n y_n).
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
\textbf{Addition.}
Let $\varepsilon > 0$.  Since $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ are Cauchy, there exist
$N_1, N_2 \in \mathbb{N}$ such that
$|x_m - x_n| < \varepsilon/2$ for all $m,n \geq N_1$ and
$|y_m - y_n| < \varepsilon/2$ for all $m,n \geq N_2$.
Set $N = \max\{N_1, N_2\}$.  For $m, n \geq N$:
\[
|(x_m + y_m) - (x_n + y_n)|
\leq |x_m - x_n| + |y_m - y_n|
< \frac{\varepsilon}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon}{2} = \varepsilon.
\]

\textbf{Multiplication.}
By \hyperref[thm:cauchy-bounded-q]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:cauchy-bounded-q}},
both sequences are bounded; let $\Lambda > 0$ satisfy $|x_n| \leq \Lambda$
and $|y_n| \leq \Lambda$ for all $n$.
Let $\varepsilon > 0$.  Choose $N_1, N_2$ such that
$|x_m - x_n| < \varepsilon/(2\Lambda)$ for $m,n \geq N_1$ and
$|y_m - y_n| < \varepsilon/(2\Lambda)$ for $m,n \geq N_2$.
Set $N = \max\{N_1, N_2\}$.  For $m, n \geq N$:
\[
|x_m y_m - x_n y_n|
= |x_m y_m - x_n y_m + x_n y_m - x_n y_n|
\leq |y_m||x_m - x_n| + |x_n||y_m - y_n|
< \Lambda \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{2\Lambda} + \Lambda \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{2\Lambda}
= \varepsilon.
\]
nodes.proof_idea:
Both parts are $\varepsilon$-splitting arguments.  The addition case is a
direct two-term triangle inequality.  The multiplication case adds one
layer of difficulty: the product does not split cleanly, so one must first
algebraically separate the two sources of error (the $x$-difference and
the $y$-difference) using the cross-term trick, then compensate for the
bounds $\Lambda$ by scaling the tolerance down to $\varepsilon/(2\Lambda)$
before extracting the Cauchy witnesses.  This $\Lambda$-compensation pattern
recurs throughout analysis wherever products of sequences appear.
nodes.source_text:
For addition: an $\varepsilon/2$ split suffices. For multiplication: Cauchy
sequences are bounded, so cross-terms $|y_n||x_n - x_m|$ and
$|x_m||y_n - y_m|$ are controlled by a common bound $\Lambda$, and
$\varepsilon/(2\Lambda)$ serves as the input tolerance for each factor.

nodes.name: Equivalence of Cauchy sequences in $ $
nodes.question: State the definition: Equivalence of Cauchy sequences in $ $.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\mathbb{Q}^c$ denote the set of all Cauchy sequences of rational numbers.
Two sequences $(x_n), (y_n) \in \mathbb{Q}^c$ are \emph{equivalent}, written
$(x_n) \sim (y_n)$, if
\[
\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\; \exists N \in \mathbb{N} \;\; \forall n \geq N,
\quad |x_n - y_n| < \varepsilon.
\]
Equivalently, $(x_n) \sim (y_n)$ if and only if $(x_n - y_n)$ converges to $0$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $\mathbb{Q}^c$ denote the set of all Cauchy sequences of rational numbers.
Two sequences $(x_n), (y_n) \in \mathbb{Q}^c$ are \emph{equivalent}, written
$(x_n) \sim (y_n)$, if
\[
\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\; \exists N \in \mathbb{N} \;\; \forall n \geq N,
\quad |x_n - y_n| < \varepsilon.
\]
Equivalently, $(x_n) \sim (y_n)$ if and only if $(x_n - y_n)$ converges to $0$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\operatorname{CauchyEquiv}(x_n, y_n)
\;\coloneqq\;
\operatorname{ConvergesTo}(x_n - y_n,\, 0).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\operatorname{CauchyEquiv}(x_n, y_n)
\;\coloneqq\;
\operatorname{ConvergesTo}(x_n - y_n,\, 0).
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\neg\operatorname{CauchyEquiv}(x_n, y_n)
\;\coloneqq\;
\exists \varepsilon > 0 \;\; \forall N \in \mathbb{N} \;\; \exists n \geq N,
\quad |x_n - y_n| \geq \varepsilon.
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\neg\operatorname{CauchyEquiv}(x_n, y_n)
\;\coloneqq\;
\exists \varepsilon > 0 \;\; \forall N \in \mathbb{N} \;\; \exists n \geq N,
\quad |x_n - y_n| \geq \varepsilon.
\]
nodes.source_text:
Two Cauchy sequences are equivalent when their difference is a null sequence.
The equivalence classes under $\sim$ are exactly the real numbers in the
Cauchy-sequence construction of $\mathbb{R}$.

nodes.name: The relation $ $ is an equivalence relation on $ ^c$
nodes.question: State the theorem: The relation $ $ is an equivalence relation on $ ^c$.
nodes.statement_display:
The relation $\sim$ defined on $\mathbb{Q}^c$ by
$(x_n) \sim (y_n) \Longleftrightarrow \operatorname{ConvergesTo}(x_n - y_n, 0)$
is an equivalence relation.
nodes.statement_tex:
The relation $\sim$ defined on $\mathbb{Q}^c$ by
$(x_n) \sim (y_n) \Longleftrightarrow \operatorname{ConvergesTo}(x_n - y_n, 0)$
is an equivalence relation.
nodes.formal:
The relation $\sim$ on $\mathbb{Q}^c$ satisfies:
\begin{align*}
&\forall (x_n) \in \mathbb{Q}^c,\quad (x_n) \sim (x_n); \\
&\forall (x_n),(y_n) \in \mathbb{Q}^c,\quad
  (x_n) \sim (y_n) \Longrightarrow (y_n) \sim (x_n); \\
&\forall (x_n),(y_n),(z_n) \in \mathbb{Q}^c,\quad
  (x_n) \sim (y_n) \land (y_n) \sim (z_n)
  \Longrightarrow (x_n) \sim (z_n).
\end{align*}
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
The relation $\sim$ on $\mathbb{Q}^c$ satisfies:
\begin{align*}
&\forall (x_n) \in \mathbb{Q}^c,\quad (x_n) \sim (x_n); \\
&\forall (x_n),(y_n) \in \mathbb{Q}^c,\quad
  (x_n) \sim (y_n) \Longrightarrow (y_n) \sim (x_n); \\
&\forall (x_n),(y_n),(z_n) \in \mathbb{Q}^c,\quad
  (x_n) \sim (y_n) \land (y_n) \sim (z_n)
  \Longrightarrow (x_n) \sim (z_n).
\end{align*}
nodes.proof_sketch:
\textbf{Reflexivity.}
For any $(x_n) \in \mathbb{Q}^c$: $|x_n - x_n| = 0 < \varepsilon$ for all
$\varepsilon > 0$ and all $n$, so $(x_n) \sim (x_n)$.

\textbf{Symmetry.}
If $(x_n) \sim (y_n)$ then $|x_n - y_n| \to 0$.
Since $|y_n - x_n| = |x_n - y_n|$, we have $|y_n - x_n| \to 0$, so
$(y_n) \sim (x_n)$.

\textbf{Transitivity.}
Suppose $(x_n) \sim (y_n)$ and $(y_n) \sim (z_n)$.
Let $\varepsilon > 0$.  There exist $N_1, N_2 \in \mathbb{N}$ such that
$|x_n - y_n| < \varepsilon/2$ for $n \geq N_1$ and
$|y_n - z_n| < \varepsilon/2$ for $n \geq N_2$.
For all $n \geq \max\{N_1, N_2\}$:
\[
|x_n - z_n|
\leq |x_n - y_n| + |y_n - z_n|
< \frac{\varepsilon}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon}{2} = \varepsilon.
\]
Therefore $(x_n) \sim (z_n)$.
nodes.proof_idea:
This proof is a direct three-part verification.  Reflexivity is trivial.
Symmetry follows from $|a - b| = |b - a|$.  Transitivity is a
$2\varepsilon$ split via the triangle inequality — structurally identical
to the proof of uniqueness of limits and the proof that convergent
sequences are Cauchy.  All three proofs share a single master template.
nodes.source_text:
Reflexivity: $|x_n - x_n| = 0 < \varepsilon$ always.
Symmetry: $|x_n - y_n| = |y_n - x_n|$.
Transitivity: the triangle inequality with an $\varepsilon/2$ split.
depends_on_titles: Cauchy sequences are closed under addition and multiplication

nodes.name: Cauchy equivalence is preserved under addition and multiplication
nodes.question: State the theorem: Cauchy equivalence is preserved under addition and multiplication.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n),(y_n),(u_n),(v_n) \in \mathbb{Q}^c$.
Suppose $(x_n) \sim (u_n)$ and $(y_n) \sim (v_n)$. Then:
\[
(x_n + y_n) \sim (u_n + v_n)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
(x_n y_n) \sim (u_n v_n).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n),(y_n),(u_n),(v_n) \in \mathbb{Q}^c$.
Suppose $(x_n) \sim (u_n)$ and $(y_n) \sim (v_n)$. Then:
\[
(x_n + y_n) \sim (u_n + v_n)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
(x_n y_n) \sim (u_n v_n).
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
(x_n) \sim (u_n) \land (y_n) \sim (v_n)
\;\Longrightarrow\;
(x_n + y_n) \sim (u_n + v_n)
\;\land\;
(x_n y_n) \sim (u_n v_n).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
(x_n) \sim (u_n) \land (y_n) \sim (v_n)
\;\Longrightarrow\;
(x_n + y_n) \sim (u_n + v_n)
\;\land\;
(x_n y_n) \sim (u_n v_n).
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
\textbf{Addition.}
Let $\varepsilon > 0$.  Since $(x_n) \sim (u_n)$, there exists $N_1$ with
$|x_n - u_n| < \varepsilon/2$ for $n \geq N_1$.
Since $(y_n) \sim (v_n)$, there exists $N_2$ with
$|y_n - v_n| < \varepsilon/2$ for $n \geq N_2$.
For $n \geq \max\{N_1, N_2\}$:
\[
|(x_n + y_n) - (u_n + v_n)|
\leq |x_n - u_n| + |y_n - v_n|
< \frac{\varepsilon}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon}{2} = \varepsilon.
\]

\textbf{Multiplication.}
By \hyperref[thm:cauchy-bounded-q]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:cauchy-bounded-q}},
all four sequences are bounded; let $\Lambda > 0$ satisfy $|x_n|, |y_n|, |u_n|, |v_n| \leq \Lambda$.
Let $\varepsilon > 0$.  Choose $N_1$ with $|x_n - u_n| < \varepsilon/(2\Lambda)$ for $n \geq N_1$,
and $N_2$ with $|y_n - v_n| < \varepsilon/(2\Lambda)$ for $n \geq N_2$.
Write:
\[
x_n y_n - u_n v_n
= (x_n - u_n)y_n + u_n(y_n - v_n).
\]
For $n \geq \max\{N_1, N_2\}$:
\[
|x_n y_n - u_n v_n|
\leq |y_n||x_n - u_n| + |u_n||y_n - v_n|
< \Lambda \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{2\Lambda} + \Lambda \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{2\Lambda}
= \varepsilon.
\]
nodes.proof_idea:
This is a well-definedness proof.  The addition part is a direct triangle
inequality split.  The multiplication part uses the same cross-term
decomposition as in the closure-under-multiplication proof, but now the
two sequences being ``moved'' are the equivalence hypotheses $(x_n - u_n)$
and $(y_n - v_n)$, while $\Lambda$ bounds the coefficients $y_n$ and $u_n$.
The $\Lambda$-compensation pattern is the same in both proofs.
nodes.source_text:
This is the well-definedness result for arithmetic on equivalence classes.
Without it, adding or multiplying representatives from two classes could
land in different classes, making the operations on $\mathbb{R} = \mathbb{Q}^c/\!\sim$
ill-defined. The proof uses boundedness of Cauchy sequences in the same
way as the analogous well-definedness result for multiplication on $\mathbb{Q}$.
depends_on_titles: Cauchy sequences are closed under addition and multiplication

nodes.name: Rational Square Bracketing
nodes.question: State the theorem: Rational Square Bracketing.
nodes.statement_display:
For every $r\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $r>0$, there exist $a,b\in\mathbb{Q}$ with
$a>0$ and $b>0$ such that
\[
a^2<r<b^2.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
For every $r\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $r>0$, there exist $a,b\in\mathbb{Q}$ with
$a>0$ and $b>0$ such that
\[
a^2<r<b^2.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
Let
\[
a:=\frac{r}{r+1}
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
b:=r+1.
\]
Since $r\in\mathbb{Q}$ and $r>0$, both $a$ and $b$ are rational and positive.

We first show that $a^2<r$. Because $r>0$, we have $r+1>1$, hence
\[
0<a=\frac{r}{r+1}<r.
\]
Also $a<1$, since
\[
\frac{r}{r+1}<1.
\]
Multiplying the inequality $a<1$ by the positive number $a$ gives
\[
a^2<a.
\]
Since $a<r$, it follows that
\[
a^2<r.
\]

Now we show that $r<b^2$. Since $b=r+1$ and $r>0$, we have
\[
b=r+1>r.
\]
Because $b>1>0$, multiplying the inequality $b>1$ by the positive number $b$
gives
\[
b^2>b.
\]
Combining this with $b>r$, we obtain
\[
r<b^2.
\]

Therefore there exist positive rational numbers $a$ and $b$ such that
\[
a^2<r<b^2,
\]
as required.
nodes.proof_idea:
The proof uses an explicit construction rather than an existence argument by
approximation. The lower bracket is chosen as
\[
a=\frac{r}{r+1},
\]
which is both positive and strictly less than $1$ and $r$, so its square is
even smaller. The upper bracket is chosen as
\[
b=r+1,
\]
which is larger than $r$ and larger than $1$, so its square is larger still.
This produces rational squares on both sides of the given positive rational.
nodes.source_text:
Every positive rational lies strictly between the squares of two positive
rationals. The witnesses are $a = r/(r+1)$ and $b = r+1$: one checks
$a^2 < r$ and $r < b^2$ directly from the ordered field arithmetic of
$\mathbb{Q}$.
nodes.text_preview: [Rational Square Bracketing] For every $r $ with $r>0$, there exist $a,b $ with $a>0$ and $b>0$ such that \[ a^2<r<b^2. \]

nodes.name: First Grid Point Crossing
nodes.question: State the lemma: First Grid Point Crossing.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $r\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $r>0$ and let $q\in\mathbb{N}$ with $q\ge 1$.
If $K\in\mathbb{N}$ satisfies $K^2>r$, then the set
\[
A=\{m\in\mathbb{N}:m^2>rq^2\}
\]
is nonempty and therefore has a least element.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $r\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $r>0$ and let $q\in\mathbb{N}$ with $q\ge 1$.
If $K\in\mathbb{N}$ satisfies $K^2>r$, then the set
\[
A=\{m\in\mathbb{N}:m^2>rq^2\}
\]
is nonempty and therefore has a least element.
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall r\in\mathbb{Q},\;r>0\;\;
\forall q\in\mathbb{N},\;q\ge1\;\;
\bigl(\exists K\in\mathbb{N}\;(K^2>r)\bigr)
\Longrightarrow
\exists p_0\in\mathbb{N}\;\bigl(p_0\in A \land \forall m\in A\;(p_0\le m)\bigr).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall r\in\mathbb{Q},\;r>0\;\;
\forall q\in\mathbb{N},\;q\ge1\;\;
\bigl(\exists K\in\mathbb{N}\;(K^2>r)\bigr)
\Longrightarrow
\exists p_0\in\mathbb{N}\;\bigl(p_0\in A \land \forall m\in A\;(p_0\le m)\bigr).
\]
nodes.source_text:
The set $A$ is nonempty because $Kq \in A$. By the Well-Ordering Principle,
every nonempty subset of $\mathbb{N}$ has a least element. That least element
$p$ is the first grid point $p/q$ whose square exceeds $r$.
nodes.text_preview: [First Grid Point Crossing] Let $r $ with $r>0$ and let $q $ with $q 1$. If $K $ satisfies $K^2>r$, then the set \[ A=\ m :m^2>rq^2\ \] is nonempty and therefore has a least element.

nodes.name: Rational Square Approximation from Above
nodes.question: State the theorem: Rational Square Approximation from Above.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $r\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $r>0$, and let $n\in\mathbb{N}$ with $n\ge 1$.
Then there exists $s\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $s>0$ such that
\[
r<s^2<r+\frac1n.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $r\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $r>0$, and let $n\in\mathbb{N}$ with $n\ge 1$.
Then there exists $s\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $s>0$ such that
\[
r<s^2<r+\frac1n.
\]
nodes.source_text:
Choose grid denominator $q$ large enough that $2Kq^{-1} < 1/n$ where $K$ is a
rational upper bound for $\sqrt{r}$. Let $p$ be the least natural number with
$p^2 > rq^2$ and set $s = p/q$. Then $s^2 > r$ by construction and
$s^2 - r = (p^2 - rq^2)/q^2 \le (2p-1)/q^2 < 2K/q < 1/n$.
nodes.text_preview: [Rational Square Approximation from Above] Let $r $ with $r>0$, and let $n $ with $n 1$. Then there exists $s $ with $s>0$ such that \[ r<s^2<r+ 1n. \]

nodes.name: Rational Square Approximation from Below
nodes.question: State the theorem: Rational Square Approximation from Below.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $r\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $r>0$, and let $n\in\mathbb{N}$ with $n\ge 1$.
If $r-1/n>0$, then there exists $s\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $s>0$ such that
\[
r-\frac1n<s^2<r.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $r\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $r>0$, and let $n\in\mathbb{N}$ with $n\ge 1$.
If $r-1/n>0$, then there exists $s\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $s>0$ such that
\[
r-\frac1n<s^2<r.
\]
nodes.source_text:
The rational $r - 1/n$ is positive by hypothesis. By the Approximation from
Above theorem applied to $r - 1/n$, there exists $s$ with
$(r-1/n) < s^2 < (r-1/n) + 1/n = r$.
nodes.text_preview: [Rational Square Approximation from Below] Let $r $ with $r>0$, and let $n $ with $n 1$. If $r-1/n>0$, then there exists $s $ with $s>0$ such that \[ r- 1n<s^2<r. \]

nodes.name: $ $ Is an Ordered Field
nodes.question: State the theorem: $ $ Is an Ordered Field.
nodes.statement_display:
With its usual order, the system
\[
(\mathbb{Q},+,\cdot,0,1,<)
\]
is an ordered field.
nodes.statement_tex:
With its usual order, the system
\[
(\mathbb{Q},+,\cdot,0,1,<)
\]
is an ordered field.
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&(\mathbb{Q},+,\cdot,0,1) \text{ is a field and } < \text{ is an order on } \mathbb{Q}\\
&\text{such that for all } a,b,c\in\mathbb{Q}:\\
&\quad (a<b \Longrightarrow a+c<b+c)\\
&\quad \land\;(a<b \land 0<c \Longrightarrow ac<bc).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&(\mathbb{Q},+,\cdot,0,1) \text{ is a field and } < \text{ is an order on } \mathbb{Q}\\
&\text{such that for all } a,b,c\in\mathbb{Q}:\\
&\quad (a<b \Longrightarrow a+c<b+c)\\
&\quad \land\;(a<b \land 0<c \Longrightarrow ac<bc).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.source_text:
This theorem records the compatibility of the rational order with the field
operations. It is this compatibility that makes inequalities in $\mathbb{Q}$
behave as expected under addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division
by positive elements.
used_by_titles: Between Any Two Rational Numbers Lies Another Rational
Limits of rational sequences are unique
nodes.text_preview: [$ $ Is an Ordered Field] With its usual order, the system \[ ( ,+, ,0,1,<) \] is an ordered field.

nodes.name: Squares Are Nonnegative
nodes.question: State the theorem: Squares Are Nonnegative.
nodes.statement_display:
In any ordered field $F$, for every $a\in F$,
\[
a^2\ge 0.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
In any ordered field $F$, for every $a\in F$,
\[
a^2\ge 0.
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall a\in F,\quad a^2\ge 0.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall a\in F,\quad a^2\ge 0.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
\textbf{Professional Standard Proof.}~\\
Let $a\in F$.  By trichotomy in the ordered field $F$, exactly one of the
following holds:
\[
a>0,\qquad a=0,\qquad -a>0.
\]

If $a=0$, then
\[
a^2=0,
\]
so certainly $a^2\ge 0$.

If $a>0$, then the product of two positive elements is positive.  Hence
\[
a\cdot a>0,
\]
that is,
\[
a^2>0.
\]
Therefore $a^2\ge 0$.

Finally, suppose $-a>0$.  Then again the product of two positive elements is
positive, so
\[
(-a)\cdot(-a)>0.
\]
By the algebraic identity for the product of negatives,
\[
(-a)\cdot(-a)=a\cdot a=a^2.
\]
Thus
\[
a^2>0,
\]
and therefore $a^2\ge 0$.

In every case, $a^2\ge 0$.
nodes.proof_idea:
The proof splits according to the trichotomy built into the order.  If
$a=0$, the conclusion is immediate.  If $a>0$, positivity of products gives
$a^2>0$.  If $-a>0$, the same positivity argument applies to $(-a)^2$, and
the field identity $(-a)(-a)=a^2$ transfers the conclusion back to $a^2$.
So the square can never land in the negative part of the order.
nodes.source_text:
No element of an ordered field has a negative square. This immediately rules
out the existence of $\sqrt{-1}$ inside any ordered field and explains why
complex numbers require abandoning the order structure.
nodes.text_preview: [Squares Are Nonnegative] In any ordered field $F$, for every $a F$, \[ a^2 0. \]

nodes.name: Positive Elements Have Positive Inverses
nodes.question: State the theorem: Positive Elements Have Positive Inverses.
nodes.statement_display: In any ordered field, if $a>0$, then $a^{-1}>0$.
nodes.statement_tex: In any ordered field, if $a>0$, then $a^{-1}>0$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall a\in F,\quad a>0 \Longrightarrow a^{-1}>0.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall a\in F,\quad a>0 \Longrightarrow a^{-1}>0.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
\textbf{Professional Standard Proof.}~\\
Let $F$ be an ordered field, and suppose that $a>0$.  Since $F$ is a field
and $a\neq 0$, the multiplicative inverse $a^{-1}$ exists.

We claim that $a^{-1}>0$.  Suppose, for contradiction, that
\[
a^{-1}<0.
\]
By adding $-a^{-1}$ to both sides, we obtain
\[
0<-a^{-1}.
\]
Now $0<a$ and $0<-a^{-1}$, so applying the order axiom for multiplication by
a positive element to the inequality
\[
0<-a^{-1}
\]
with multiplier $a$, we get
\[
0\cdot a<(-a^{-1})\cdot a.
\]
Thus
\[
0<a(-a^{-1}).
\]
Since
\[
a(-a^{-1})=-(aa^{-1})=-1,
\]
it follows that
\[
0<-1.
\]
Adding $1$ to both sides yields
\[
1<0.
\]
Now multiply the inequality $1<0$ by the positive element $a$.  Again by the
order axiom for multiplication by a positive element,
\[
1\cdot a<0\cdot a.
\]
Hence
\[
a<0,
\]
which contradicts the assumption that $a>0$.

Therefore our assumption $a^{-1}<0$ was false.  Since $a^{-1}\neq 0$, the
trichotomy law in the ordered field implies that
\[
a^{-1}>0.
\]
nodes.proof_idea:
Assume $a>0$ and argue by contradiction.  If the inverse were negative, then
its negation would be positive.  Multiplying the positive inequality
$0<-a^{-1}$ by the positive element $a$ forces $0<-1$, and then translation
gives $1<0$.  Multiplying that inequality by the same positive element $a$
produces $a<0$, contradicting $a>0$.  So the inverse cannot be negative; by
trichotomy and nonzeroness of inverses, it must be positive.
nodes.source_text:
Dividing by a positive number cannot change the direction of an inequality.
This is the formal basis for the rule that multiplying both sides of an
inequality by a positive denominator preserves the direction.
used_by_titles: Fraction Comparison When the Denominator Product Is Positive
Positivity of a Quotient via the Product $ab$
nodes.text_preview: [Positive Elements Have Positive Inverses] In any ordered field, if $a>0$, then $a^ -1 >0$.

nodes.name: Multiplication by a Positive Element Preserves Order
nodes.question: State the theorem: Multiplication by a Positive Element Preserves Order.
nodes.statement_display:
In any ordered field, if $a<b$ and $c>0$, then
\[
ac<bc.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
In any ordered field, if $a<b$ and $c>0$, then
\[
ac<bc.
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall a,b,c\in F,\quad a<b \land c>0 \Longrightarrow ac<bc.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall a,b,c\in F,\quad a<b \land c>0 \Longrightarrow ac<bc.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
\textbf{Professional Standard Proof.}~\\
Let $F$ be an ordered field, and suppose that
\[
a<b
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
c>0.
\]
By definition, an ordered field is a field equipped with an order making it
an ordered integral domain.  One of the axioms of an ordered integral domain
states precisely that whenever
\[
a<b
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
0<c,
\]
then
\[
ac<bc.
\]
Applying this axiom to the present hypotheses gives
\[
ac<bc.
\]
This is exactly the desired conclusion.
nodes.proof_idea:
This proof is an unpacking of definitions.  The theorem is not derived from
earlier algebraic lemmas; it is one of the compatibility clauses built into
the definition of an ordered integral domain, and hence into the definition
of an ordered field.  So the argument consists of recognizing that the
hypotheses match the axiom exactly and then invoking it.
nodes.source_text:
Multiplying both sides of an inequality by a positive element leaves the
direction unchanged. This is the order-field axiom itself, stated as an
independent theorem for citation.
used_by_titles: General Fraction Comparison in an Ordered Field
Fraction Comparison When the Denominator Product Is Positive
Multiplication by a Negative Element Reverses Order
Positivity of a Quotient via the Product $ab$

nodes.name: Multiplication by a Negative Element Reverses Order
nodes.question: State the theorem: Multiplication by a Negative Element Reverses Order.
nodes.statement_display:
In any ordered field, if $a<b$ and $c<0$, then
\[
ac>bc.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
In any ordered field, if $a<b$ and $c<0$, then
\[
ac>bc.
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall a,b,c\in F,\quad a<b \land c<0 \Longrightarrow ac>bc.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall a,b,c\in F,\quad a<b \land c<0 \Longrightarrow ac>bc.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
\textbf{Professional Standard Proof.}~\\
Let $F$ be an ordered field, and suppose that
\[
a<b
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
c<0.
\]
Adding $-c$ to both sides of the inequality $c<0$, we obtain
\[
0<-c.
\]
Thus $-c>0$.

By \hyperref[thm:multiply-positive]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:multiply-positive}},
since $a<b$ and $-c>0$, we have
\[
a(-c)<b(-c).
\]
Using the negation-of-product identity,
\[
a(-c)=-(ac)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
b(-c)=-(bc).
\]
Therefore
\[
-(ac)<-(bc).
\]
Add $bc$ to both sides:
\[
bc-(ac)<0.
\]
Since
\[
bc-(ac)=bc+(-(ac))=bc+(-ac)=bc-ac,
\]
adding $ac$ to both sides gives
\[
bc<ac.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
ac>bc.
\]
This is exactly the desired conclusion.
nodes.proof_idea:
The proof converts the negative multiplier into a positive one by replacing
$c$ with $-c$.  Since $-c>0$, the positive-multiplier theorem applies and
gives $a(-c)<b(-c)$.  The algebraic identity for negation of a product turns
this into $-(ac)<-(bc)$.  A final pair of additive translations removes the
leading minus signs and reverses the order into the desired form $ac>bc$.
nodes.source_text:
Multiplying both sides of an inequality by a negative element reverses the
direction. This is the formal explanation of the familiar rule that one
``flips the inequality sign'' when multiplying by a negative number.
depends_on_titles: Multiplication by a Positive Element Preserves Order
used_by_titles: General Fraction Comparison in an Ordered Field
Positivity of a Quotient via the Product $ab$

nodes.text_preview: [Multiplication by a Negative Element Reverses Order] In any ordered field, if $a<b$ and $c<0$, then \[ ac>bc. \]

nodes.name: $1$ Is Positive
nodes.question: State the theorem: $1$ Is Positive.
nodes.statement_display:
In any ordered field,
\[
1>0.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
In any ordered field,
\[
1>0.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
\textbf{Professional Standard Proof.}~\\
Let $F$ be an ordered field.  By the field axioms,
\[
1\neq 0.
\]
By \hyperref[thm:squares-nonnegative]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:squares-nonnegative}},
every square is nonnegative.  Applying this to $1$, we obtain
\[
1^2\ge 0.
\]
But
\[
1^2=1,
\]
so
\[
1\ge 0.
\]

Now trichotomy in the ordered field tells us that exactly one of the
following holds:
\[
1>0,\qquad 1=0,\qquad 1<0.
\]
Since $1\neq 0$, the middle possibility is excluded.  Since $1\ge 0$, the
possibility $1<0$ is excluded as well.  Therefore the only remaining
possibility is
\[
1>0.
\]
nodes.proof_idea:
The proof packages the desired conclusion into the identity $1=1^2$.  Since
every square is nonnegative, $1$ must be nonnegative.  But the multiplicative
identity in a field is nonzero, so trichotomy rules out the possibility
$1=0$.  A nonnegative element that is not zero must be positive, which
forces $1>0$.
nodes.source_text:
The multiplicative identity of any ordered field is positive. This anchors
the order structure: every natural number $n$ embedded in the field via
repeated addition of $1$ is also positive.
used_by_titles: Between Any Two Rational Numbers Lies Another Rational
nodes.text_preview: [$1$ Is Positive] In any ordered field, \[ 1>0. \]

nodes.name: Natural Numbers Are Positive
nodes.question: State the corollary: Natural Numbers Are Positive.
nodes.statement_display:
In any ordered field, for every natural number $n$,
\[
n>0.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
In any ordered field, for every natural number $n$,
\[
n>0.
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall n\in\mathbb{N},\quad n>0.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall n\in\mathbb{N},\quad n>0.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
\textbf{Professional Standard Proof.}~\\
Let $F$ be an ordered field.  We prove that every natural number, viewed in
$F$ by the usual embedding, is positive.

By \hyperref[thm:one-positive]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:one-positive}},
\[
1>0.
\]

Now every natural number is obtained by starting from $1$ and repeatedly
adding $1$.  We prove by induction on $n\in\mathbb{N}$ that the
corresponding element of $F$ is positive.

\emph{Base case.}  For $n=1$, the claim is exactly
\[
1>0.
\]

\emph{Inductive step.}  Assume $n>0$ for some natural number $n$.  Since
\[
1>0,
\]
and the positive part of an ordered field is closed under addition, we have
\[
n+1>0.
\]

Therefore, by induction, every natural number $n$ satisfies
\[
n>0.
\]
nodes.proof_idea:
The proof starts from the positivity of the multiplicative identity and then
propagates that positivity through the natural numbers by repeated addition.
The key closure property is that the sum of two positive elements is again
positive.  Since the natural numbers are built by iterating the successor
operation $n\mapsto n+1$, induction transports the positivity of $1$ to all
of $\mathbb{N}$.
nodes.source_text:
Every natural number, when embedded in an ordered field by repeated addition
of the identity $1$, is a positive element of the field. This is the
foundational fact behind the Archimedean property.
used_by_titles: Denominator growth on infinite Stern--Brocot paths
nodes.text_preview: [Natural Numbers Are Positive] In any ordered field, for every natural number $n$, \[ n>0. \]

nodes.name: Positivity of a Quotient via the Product $ab$
nodes.question: State the theorem: Positivity of a Quotient via the Product $ab$.
nodes.statement_display:
For any $a,b\in F$ with $b\neq 0$,
\[
0<\frac{a}{b}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
0<a\cdot b.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
For any $a,b\in F$ with $b\neq 0$,
\[
0<\frac{a}{b}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
0<a\cdot b.
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall a,b\in F,\quad b\neq 0
\Longrightarrow
\Bigl(0<\tfrac{a}{b} \Longleftrightarrow 0<ab\Bigr).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall a,b\in F,\quad b\neq 0
\Longrightarrow
\Bigl(0<\tfrac{a}{b} \Longleftrightarrow 0<ab\Bigr).
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
\textbf{Professional Standard Proof.}~\\
Let $a,b\in F$ with $b\neq 0$.

\emph{($\Rightarrow$)} Assume
\[
0<\frac{a}{b}.
\]
By trichotomy, since $b\neq 0$, exactly one of the two possibilities
\[
b>0
\qquad\text{or}\qquad
b<0
\]
holds.

If $b>0$, then by
\hyperref[thm:multiply-positive]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:multiply-positive}},
multiplying the inequality
\[
0<\frac{a}{b}
\]
by the positive element $b$ gives
\[
0\cdot b<\frac{a}{b}\cdot b.
\]
Hence
\[
0<a.
\]
Multiplying again by the positive element $b$, we obtain
\[
0<a\cdot b.
\]

If $b<0$, then by
\hyperref[thm:multiply-negative]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:multiply-negative}},
multiplying the inequality
\[
0<\frac{a}{b}
\]
by the negative element $b$ reverses the order:
\[
0\cdot b>\frac{a}{b}\cdot b.
\]
Thus
\[
0>a,
\]
so $a<0$.  Since also $b<0$, the product of two negative elements is
positive.  Therefore
\[
0<a\cdot b.
\]

In either case,
\[
0<a\cdot b.
\]

\emph{($\Leftarrow$)} Assume
\[
0<a\cdot b.
\]
Again, since $b\neq 0$, either $b>0$ or $b<0$.

If $b>0$, then
\hyperref[thm:inverse-positive]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:inverse-positive}}
gives
\[
b^{-1}>0.
\]
Applying
\hyperref[thm:multiply-positive]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:multiply-positive}}
to
\[
0<a\cdot b
\]
with positive multiplier $b^{-1}$ yields
\[
0<(a\cdot b)b^{-1}.
\]
By associativity,
\[
(a\cdot b)b^{-1}=a(bb^{-1})=a.
\]
Hence
\[
0<a.
\]
Multiplying by the positive element $b^{-1}$ once more, we obtain
\[
0<ab^{-1}=\frac{a}{b}.
\]

If $b<0$, then $-b>0$, so by
\hyperref[thm:inverse-positive]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:inverse-positive}},
\[
(-b)^{-1}>0.
\]
Applying
\hyperref[thm:multiply-positive]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:multiply-positive}}
to
\[
0<a\cdot b
\]
with positive multiplier $(-b)^{-1}$ gives
\[
0<(a\cdot b)(-b)^{-1}.
\]
Since
\[
b(-b)^{-1}=-1,
\]
we have
\[
(a\cdot b)(-b)^{-1}=a\bigl(b(-b)^{-1}\bigr)=a(-1)=-a.
\]
Thus
\[
0<-a,
\]
so $a<0$.

Now both $a$ and $b$ are negative.  We compare $\frac{a}{b}$ and
$\frac{-a}{-b}$ using the definition of division.  Since
\[
b\cdot\frac{a}{b}=a,
\]
the negation-of-product identity gives
\[
(-b)\cdot\frac{a}{b}
=
-\left(b\cdot\frac{a}{b}\right)
=
-a.
\]
Thus $\frac{a}{b}$ is an element whose product with $-b$ is $-a$.  By the
definition of division, the unique such element is $\frac{-a}{-b}$.  Hence
\[
\frac{a}{b}=\frac{-a}{-b}.
\]
Since $-a>0$ and $-b>0$, the first case already proved implies
\[
0<\frac{-a}{-b}.
\]
Therefore
\[
0<\frac{a}{b}.
\]

Thus
\[
0<\frac{a}{b}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
0<a\cdot b.
\]
nodes.proof_idea:
The proof splits according to the sign of the denominator.  For the forward
direction, a positive quotient is multiplied by the denominator.  If the
denominator is positive, positivity is preserved and one gets $a>0$; if the
denominator is negative, order reverses and one gets $a<0$.  In either case,
$a$ and $b$ have the same sign, so their product is positive.

For the reverse direction, start from $ab>0$ and again inspect the sign of
$b$.  If $b>0$, multiplying by the positive inverse $b^{-1}$ shows first that
$a>0$ and then that $a/b>0$.  If $b<0$, multiplying by the positive inverse
$(-b)^{-1}$ shows that $-a>0$, so both $-a$ and $-b$ are positive; the
positive-denominator case then applies to $(-a)/(-b)$, and the quotient is
identified with $a/b$ by a sign-change identity.
nodes.uses_text: def:ordered-field, thm:inverse-positive, thm:multiply-positive, thm:multiply-negative, thm:negation-of-product, cor:product-of-negatives
nodes.source_text:
A fraction is positive exactly when numerator and denominator have the same
sign — equivalently, when their product is positive. This reduces sign-testing
of fractions to sign-testing of products.
depends_on_titles: Multiplication by a Negative Element Reverses Order
Multiplication by a Positive Element Preserves Order
used_by_titles: General Fraction Comparison in an Ordered Field

nodes.name: General Fraction Comparison in an Ordered Field
nodes.question: State the theorem: General Fraction Comparison in an Ordered Field.
nodes.statement_display:
For any $a,b,c,d\in F$ with $b\neq 0$ and $d\neq 0$,
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
(a\cdot d)\cdot(b\cdot d)<(b\cdot c)\cdot(b\cdot d).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
For any $a,b,c,d\in F$ with $b\neq 0$ and $d\neq 0$,
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
(a\cdot d)\cdot(b\cdot d)<(b\cdot c)\cdot(b\cdot d).
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall a,b,c,d\in F,\quad b\neq 0\land d\neq 0
\Longrightarrow
\Bigl(\tfrac{a}{b}<\tfrac{c}{d}
\Longleftrightarrow (ad)(bd)<(bc)(bd)\Bigr).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall a,b,c,d\in F,\quad b\neq 0\land d\neq 0
\Longrightarrow
\Bigl(\tfrac{a}{b}<\tfrac{c}{d}
\Longleftrightarrow (ad)(bd)<(bc)(bd)\Bigr).
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
\textbf{Professional Standard Proof.}~\\
Let $a,b,c,d\in F$ with $b\neq 0$ and $d\neq 0$.  Since $F$ is a field,
\[
b\cdot d\neq 0.
\]

We begin by rewriting the comparison of the two quotients as a positivity
statement about their difference:
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
0<\frac{c}{d}-\frac{a}{b}.
\]

Set
\[
x:=\frac{c}{d}-\frac{a}{b}.
\]
We claim that
\[
x=\frac{b\cdot c-a\cdot d}{b\cdot d}.
\]
To verify this, multiply $x$ by $b\cdot d$:
\[
(b\cdot d)x
=(b\cdot d)\left(\frac{c}{d}-\frac{a}{b}\right).
\]
Distributing and regrouping,
\[
(b\cdot d)x
=(b\cdot d)\frac{c}{d}-(b\cdot d)\frac{a}{b}
=b\left(d\frac{c}{d}\right)-d\left(b\frac{a}{b}\right).
\]
By the defining property of division,
\[
d\frac{c}{d}=c
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
b\frac{a}{b}=a.
\]
Hence
\[
(b\cdot d)x=b\cdot c-a\cdot d.
\]
By the definition of division, the unique element whose product with
$b\cdot d$ is $b\cdot c-a\cdot d$ is
\[
\frac{b\cdot c-a\cdot d}{b\cdot d}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\frac{c}{d}-\frac{a}{b}=\frac{b\cdot c-a\cdot d}{b\cdot d}.
\]

Substituting this into the inequality above gives
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
0<\frac{b\cdot c-a\cdot d}{b\cdot d}.
\]
Now apply
\hyperref[thm:positive-fraction-iff-positive-product]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:positive-fraction-iff-positive-product}}
with numerator $b\cdot c-a\cdot d$ and denominator $b\cdot d$:
\[
0<\frac{b\cdot c-a\cdot d}{b\cdot d}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
0<(b\cdot c-a\cdot d)\cdot(b\cdot d).
\]
Distributing the product on the right,
\[
0<(b\cdot c)\cdot(b\cdot d)-(a\cdot d)\cdot(b\cdot d).
\]
Adding $(a\cdot d)\cdot(b\cdot d)$ to both sides, we obtain
\[
(a\cdot d)\cdot(b\cdot d)<(b\cdot c)\cdot(b\cdot d).
\]

Combining the equivalences yields
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
(a\cdot d)\cdot(b\cdot d)<(b\cdot c)\cdot(b\cdot d),
\]
as required.
nodes.proof_idea:
The proof converts the comparison of two quotients into a positivity statement
for their difference.  That difference is then rewritten as a single quotient
with denominator $bd$ by checking directly that multiplying by $bd$ recovers
the numerator $bc-ad$.  Once the expression has been packed into one quotient,
the earlier theorem characterizing positivity of a quotient by the sign of its
numerator-times-denominator applies immediately.  A final additive
rearrangement turns the positivity condition into the comparison
$(ad)(bd)<(bc)(bd)$.
nodes.source_text:
This cross-multiplication criterion for fraction comparison is valid for
arbitrary nonzero denominators, regardless of sign. It reduces fraction order
to the order of products, which is controlled by the sign-arithmetic theorems.
depends_on_titles: Multiplication by a Negative Element Reverses Order
Multiplication by a Positive Element Preserves Order
Positivity of a Quotient via the Product $ab$

proof_file_blocks.title: General Fraction Comparison in an Ordered Field

nodes.text_preview: [General Fraction Comparison in an Ordered Field] For any $a,b,c,d F$ with $b 0$ and $d 0$, \[ b < d (a d) (b d)<(b c) (b d). \]

nodes.name: Fraction Comparison When the Denominator Product Is Positive
nodes.question: State the theorem: Fraction Comparison When the Denominator Product Is Positive.
nodes.statement_display:
For any $a,b,c,d\in F$, if $b\neq 0$, $d\neq 0$, and $0<b\cdot d$, then
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
a\cdot d<b\cdot c.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
For any $a,b,c,d\in F$, if $b\neq 0$, $d\neq 0$, and $0<b\cdot d$, then
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
a\cdot d<b\cdot c.
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall a,b,c,d\in F,\quad b\neq 0\land d\neq 0\land 0<bd
\Longrightarrow
\Bigl(\tfrac{a}{b}<\tfrac{c}{d} \Longleftrightarrow ad<bc\Bigr).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall a,b,c,d\in F,\quad b\neq 0\land d\neq 0\land 0<bd
\Longrightarrow
\Bigl(\tfrac{a}{b}<\tfrac{c}{d} \Longleftrightarrow ad<bc\Bigr).
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
\textbf{Professional Standard Proof.}~\\
Let $a,b,c,d\in F$ with
\[
b\neq 0,\qquad d\neq 0,\qquad 0<b\cdot d.
\]
By
\hyperref[thm:fraction-order-comparison-general]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:fraction-order-comparison-general}},
we have
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
(a\cdot d)\cdot(b\cdot d)<(b\cdot c)\cdot(b\cdot d).
\]
Thus it remains to show that, under the hypothesis $0<b\cdot d$, the latter
inequality is equivalent to
\[
a\cdot d<b\cdot c.
\]

Since $0<b\cdot d$, multiplication by the positive element $b\cdot d$
preserves order.  Therefore, if
\[
a\cdot d<b\cdot c,
\]
then by
\hyperref[thm:multiply-positive]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:multiply-positive}},
\[
(a\cdot d)\cdot(b\cdot d)<(b\cdot c)\cdot(b\cdot d).
\]

Conversely, suppose that
\[
(a\cdot d)\cdot(b\cdot d)<(b\cdot c)\cdot(b\cdot d).
\]
Because $b\cdot d>0$, its inverse is positive by
\hyperref[thm:inverse-positive]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:inverse-positive}}:
\[
(b\cdot d)^{-1}>0.
\]
Applying
\hyperref[thm:multiply-positive]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:multiply-positive}}
to the displayed inequality with positive multiplier $(b\cdot d)^{-1}$, we
obtain
\[
\bigl((a\cdot d)\cdot(b\cdot d)\bigr)(b\cdot d)^{-1}
<
\bigl((b\cdot c)\cdot(b\cdot d)\bigr)(b\cdot d)^{-1}.
\]
Associativity gives
\[
(a\cdot d)\bigl((b\cdot d)(b\cdot d)^{-1}\bigr)
<
(b\cdot c)\bigl((b\cdot d)(b\cdot d)^{-1}\bigr),
\]
so
\[
(a\cdot d)\cdot 1<(b\cdot c)\cdot 1.
\]
Hence
\[
a\cdot d<b\cdot c.
\]

Combining the two equivalences,
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
a\cdot d<b\cdot c.
\]
nodes.proof_idea:
The proof starts from the general fraction-comparison theorem, which gives an
equivalent inequality after multiplying both sides by the common factor $bd$.
When $bd$ is positive, that extra factor does not disturb the order: one
direction follows immediately by multiplying by a positive element, and the
other direction is recovered by multiplying by the positive inverse
$(bd)^{-1}$.  So the theorem is the positive-denominator-product simplification
of the general comparison law.
nodes.source_text:
When the denominator product is positive — in particular when both denominators
are positive — fraction comparison reduces to the familiar cross-multiplication
rule: $a/b < c/d$ iff $ad < bc$.
depends_on_titles: General Fraction Comparison in an Ordered Field
Multiplication by a Positive Element Preserves Order

nodes.name: Reciprocal Order for Positive Elements
nodes.question: State the theorem: Reciprocal Order for Positive Elements.
nodes.statement_display:
In any ordered field, if $0<d<b$, then
\[
0<\frac{1}{b}<\frac{1}{d}.
\]
Conversely, if $0<1/b<1/d$, then $0<d<b$.
nodes.statement_tex:
In any ordered field, if $0<d<b$, then
\[
0<\frac{1}{b}<\frac{1}{d}.
\]
Conversely, if $0<1/b<1/d$, then $0<d<b$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall b,d\in F,\quad
0<d<b \Longleftrightarrow 0<\tfrac{1}{b}<\tfrac{1}{d}.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall b,d\in F,\quad
0<d<b \Longleftrightarrow 0<\tfrac{1}{b}<\tfrac{1}{d}.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
\textbf{Professional Standard Proof.}~\\
\emph{Forward direction.} Assume
\[
0<d<b.
\]
Then in particular
\[
d>0
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
b>0.
\]
By \hyperref[thm:inverse-positive]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:inverse-positive}},
the inverses of positive elements are positive.  Hence
\[
0<\frac{1}{d}
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
0<\frac{1}{b}.
\]

It remains to compare the two reciprocals.  Since $b>0$ and $d>0$, we have
\[
0<b\cdot d.
\]
Now apply
\hyperref[thm:fraction-order-comparison-positive-denominator-product]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:fraction-order-comparison-positive-denominator-product}}
with numerators $1$ and $1$:
\[
\frac{1}{b}<\frac{1}{d}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
1\cdot d<b\cdot 1.
\]
But
\[
1\cdot d=d
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
b\cdot 1=b,
\]
so the right-hand inequality is exactly
\[
d<b,
\]
which holds by hypothesis.  Therefore
\[
\frac{1}{b}<\frac{1}{d}.
\]
Combining this with $0<1/b$, we obtain
\[
0<\frac{1}{b}<\frac{1}{d}.
\]

\emph{Converse direction.} Assume
\[
0<\frac{1}{b}<\frac{1}{d}.
\]
From
\[
0<\frac{1}{b},
\]
\hyperref[thm:positive-fraction-iff-positive-product]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:positive-fraction-iff-positive-product}}
with numerator $1$ gives
\[
0<1\cdot b=b.
\]
So $b>0$.  Similarly, from
\[
0<\frac{1}{d},
\]
the same theorem gives
\[
0<1\cdot d=d,
\]
so $d>0$.

Thus
\[
0<b\cdot d.
\]
Applying
\hyperref[thm:fraction-order-comparison-positive-denominator-product]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:fraction-order-comparison-positive-denominator-product}}
again with numerators $1$ and $1$, we obtain
\[
\frac{1}{b}<\frac{1}{d}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
1\cdot d<b\cdot 1.
\]
Hence
\[
d<b.
\]
Together with $d>0$, this yields
\[
0<d<b.
\]
nodes.proof_idea:
The proof isolates two separate issues: positivity of the reciprocals and
their relative order.  Positivity comes from the inverse-positive theorem in
the forward direction, and from the quotient-positivity criterion in the
reverse direction.  Once positivity of the denominators is known, the actual
comparison is just the positive-denominator fraction comparison theorem
applied to the special fractions $1/b$ and $1/d$.  In that specialization,
the cross-multiplied inequality collapses exactly to $d<b$.
nodes.source_text:
Taking reciprocals reverses the strict order among positive elements. Larger
positive denominators produce smaller positive reciprocals. This is the formal
basis for the rule that $1/n$ decreases as $n$ increases.
depends_on_titles: Fraction Comparison When the Denominator Product Is Positive
Multiplication by a Positive Element Preserves Order
Positivity of a Quotient via the Product $ab$
nodes.text_preview: [Reciprocal Order for Positive Elements] In any ordered field, if $0<d<b$, then \[ 0< b < d . \] Conversely, if $0<1/b<1/d$, then $0<d<b$.

nodes.name: Ordered Fields Are Densely Ordered
nodes.question: State the theorem: Ordered Fields Are Densely Ordered.
nodes.statement_display: Every ordered field is densely ordered by its order relation.
nodes.statement_tex: Every ordered field is densely ordered by its order relation.
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall a,b\in F,\quad a<b \Longrightarrow \exists c\in F\;(a<c<b).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall a,b\in F,\quad a<b \Longrightarrow \exists c\in F\;(a<c<b).
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
Let $F$ be an ordered field. To show that $(F,<)$ is densely ordered, we verify
the two conditions in
\hyperref[def:densely-ordered-system]{Definition~\ref*{def:densely-ordered-system}}.

First, because $F$ is an ordered field, its order relation makes $F$ into an
ordered integral domain. In particular, for any $x,y\in F$, exactly one of the
relations
\[
x<y,\qquad x=y,\qquad y<x
\]
holds. Thus $(F,<)$ is a simply ordered system.

Now let $x,y\in F$ satisfy $x<y$. Define
\[
z:=\frac{x+y}{2}.
\]
Since $F$ is a field and $2=1+1\neq 0$, the element $2$ has a multiplicative
inverse, so $z\in F$.

Because $x<y$, the ordered-field axioms give
\[
x+x<x+y<y+y.
\]
Since $1>0$ by
\hyperref[thm:one-positive]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:one-positive}}, we have
$2=1+1>0$, and so
\hyperref[thm:inverse-positive]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:inverse-positive}} yields
$\frac12>0$. Multiplying the displayed inequality through by $\frac12$ and using
\hyperref[thm:multiply-positive]{Theorem~\ref*{thm:multiply-positive}}, we obtain
\[
\frac{x+x}{2}<\frac{x+y}{2}<\frac{y+y}{2}.
\]
By the field laws this simplifies to
\[
x<z<y.
\]
Therefore between any two distinct elements of $F$ there lies another element
of $F$. Hence $(F,<)$ is densely ordered.
nodes.proof_idea:
To prove density, one must exhibit a point strictly between two given ordered
field elements. The natural candidate is the midpoint $(x+y)/2$. The proof
first uses the ordered-field axioms to obtain $x+x<x+y<y+y$, then multiplies
through by the positive element $1/2$ to conclude
\[
x<\frac{x+y}{2}<y.
\]
This verifies the interpolation condition required for a densely ordered
system.
nodes.source_text:
In any ordered field, between any two elements there is always a third. The
witness is $(a+b)/2$: it is rational (the field is closed under addition and
division by $2$, which equals $1+1$) and lies strictly between $a$ and $b$
by the sign-arithmetic laws. This is the abstract theorem that specializes to
the density of $\mathbb{Q}$ in itself.
depends_on_titles: Multiplication by a Positive Element Preserves Order
nodes.text_preview: [Ordered Fields Are Densely Ordered] Every ordered field is densely ordered by its order relation.

nodes.name: Addition and multiplication
nodes.question: State the definition: Addition and multiplication.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $[(a,b)],[(c,d)]\in\mathbb{Q}$, where $(a,b),(c,d)\in W$. Define
\[
[(a,b)] + [(c,d)] := [(ad+bc,bd)]
\]
and
\[
[(a,b)]\cdot[(c,d)] := [(ac,bd)].
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $[(a,b)],[(c,d)]\in\mathbb{Q}$, where $(a,b),(c,d)\in W$. Define
\[
[(a,b)] + [(c,d)] := [(ad+bc,bd)]
\]
and
\[
[(a,b)]\cdot[(c,d)] := [(ac,bd)].
\]
