nodes.name: Least Upper Bound Property
nodes.question: State the definition: Least Upper Bound Property.
nodes.statement_display: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set. The set $S$ has the \emph{Least Upper Bound Property} if and only if every nonempty subset of $S$ that is bounded above in $S$ has a supremum in $S$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set. The set $S$ has the \emph{Least Upper Bound Property} if and only if every nonempty subset of $S$ that is bounded above in $S$ has a supremum in $S$.
nodes.proposition:
\[
\operatorname{LeastUpperBoundProperty}(S)\coloneqq
\forall A\subseteq S\;
\Bigl[
\bigl(A\neq\varnothing \land \exists u\in S\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)\bigr)
\rightarrow
\exists s\in S\;\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)
\Bigr].
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }(S,\le)\text{ be an ordered set}.\\
&S\text{ has the Least Upper Bound Property}\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\forall A\subseteq S\;
\Bigl[
\bigl(A\neq\varnothing \land \exists u\in S\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)\bigr)
\rightarrow
\exists s\in S\;\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }(S,\le)\text{ be an ordered set}.\\
&S\text{ has the Least Upper Bound Property}\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\forall A\subseteq S\;
\Bigl[
\bigl(A\neq\varnothing \land \exists u\in S\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)\bigr)
\rightarrow
\exists s\in S\;\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }(S,\le)\text{ be an ordered set}.\\
&S\text{ does not have the Least Upper Bound Property}\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\exists A\subseteq S\;
\Bigl[
A\neq\varnothing
\land
\exists u\in S\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)
\land
\forall s\in S\;\neg\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }(S,\le)\text{ be an ordered set}.\\
&S\text{ does not have the Least Upper Bound Property}\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\exists A\subseteq S\;
\Bigl[
A\neq\varnothing
\land
\exists u\in S\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)
\land
\forall s\in S\;\neg\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_predicate:
\[
\neg\operatorname{LeastUpperBoundProperty}(S)\Longleftrightarrow
\exists A\subseteq S\;
\Bigl[
A\neq\varnothing
\land
\exists u\in S\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)
\land
\forall s\in S\;\neg\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)
\Bigr].
\]
nodes.failure_modes: The property fails when there is a nonempty subset of the ambient ordered set that has at least one upper bound but has no least upper bound inside that same ambient set.
nodes.source_text: The Least Upper Bound Property says that upper-bounded nonempty subsets do not point toward missing ceiling elements. Whenever the order supplies at least one upper bound, it also supplies a smallest upper bound in the same ambient set. Failure therefore indicates a hole in the ordered structure rather than a defect in the subset alone.

nodes.name: Completeness of $ $
nodes.question: State the axiom: Completeness of $ $.
nodes.statement_display: The ordered field $\mathbb{R}$ has the Least Upper Bound Property.
nodes.statement_tex: The ordered field $\mathbb{R}$ has the Least Upper Bound Property.
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\neg\operatorname{LeastUpperBoundProperty}(\mathbb{R})\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\exists A\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;
\Bigl[
A\neq\varnothing
\land
\exists u\in\mathbb{R}\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)
\land
\forall s\in\mathbb{R}\;\neg\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\neg\operatorname{LeastUpperBoundProperty}(\mathbb{R})\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\exists A\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;
\Bigl[
A\neq\varnothing
\land
\exists u\in\mathbb{R}\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)
\land
\forall s\in\mathbb{R}\;\neg\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_predicate:
\[
\neg\operatorname{LeastUpperBoundProperty}(\mathbb{R})\Longleftrightarrow
\exists A\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;
\Bigl[
A\neq\varnothing
\land
\exists u\in\mathbb{R}\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)
\land
\forall s\in\mathbb{R}\;\neg\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)
\Bigr].
\]
nodes.failure_modes: Completeness would fail if some nonempty subset of $\mathbb{R}$ were bounded above in $\mathbb{R}$ but had no supremum in $\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.source_text: The completeness axiom asserts that the real line has no order gaps of the least-upper-bound kind. Nonempty sets of real numbers that are bounded above always determine a real number sitting exactly at the least possible ceiling. This is the structural property that distinguishes $\mathbb{R}$ from ordered fields such as $\mathbb{Q}$.
nodes.text_preview: [Completeness of $ $] The ordered field $ $ has the Least Upper Bound Property.

nodes.name: Dense Subset
nodes.question: State the definition: Dense Subset.
nodes.statement_display: Let $D\subseteq S\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. The set $D$ is \emph{dense in} $S$ if and only if for every $x\in S$ and every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $d\in D$ such that $|x-d|<\varepsilon$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $D\subseteq S\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. The set $D$ is \emph{dense in} $S$ if and only if for every $x\in S$ and every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $d\in D$ such that $|x-d|<\varepsilon$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }D\subseteq S\subseteq\mathbb{R}.\\
&D\text{ is dense in }S
\Longleftrightarrow
\forall x\in S\;\forall \varepsilon>0\;\exists d\in D\;\bigl(|x-d|<\varepsilon\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }D\subseteq S\subseteq\mathbb{R}.\\
&D\text{ is dense in }S
\Longleftrightarrow
\forall x\in S\;\forall \varepsilon>0\;\exists d\in D\;\bigl(|x-d|<\varepsilon\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }D\subseteq S\subseteq\mathbb{R}.\\
&D\text{ is not dense in }S
\Longleftrightarrow
\exists x\in S\;\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall d\in D\;\bigl(|x-d|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }D\subseteq S\subseteq\mathbb{R}.\\
&D\text{ is not dense in }S
\Longleftrightarrow
\exists x\in S\;\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall d\in D\;\bigl(|x-d|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.failure_modes: Density fails when some point of $S$ has a positive-radius neighborhood that contains no point of $D$.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\neg\operatorname{DenseSubset}(D,S)
=
\underbrace{\exists x\in S}_{\text{point not approximated}}
\;\underbrace{\exists \varepsilon_0>0}_{\text{positive exclusion radius}}
\;\underbrace{\forall d\in D\;\bigl(|x-d|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr)}_{\text{no point of }D\text{ enters the neighborhood}}.
\]
nodes.source_text: Density is an approximation property inside $S$. It does not say that every point of $S$ already belongs to $D$; it says that points of $D$ occur arbitrarily close to every point of $S$. Thus $D$ can be a proper subset of $S$ while still leaving no positive-width gap inside $S$.
nodes.text_preview: [Dense Subset] Let $D S $. The set $D$ is $S$ if and only if for every $x S$ and every $ >0$ there exists $d D$ such that $|x-d|< $.

nodes.name: Order Dense Subset
nodes.question: State the definition: Order Dense Subset.
nodes.statement_display: Let $X$ be a linearly ordered set and let $A\subseteq X$. The subset $A$ is \emph{order dense in} $X$ if and only if for every $a,b\in X$ with $a<b$ there exists $c\in A$ such that $a<c<b$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $X$ be a linearly ordered set and let $A\subseteq X$. The subset $A$ is \emph{order dense in} $X$ if and only if for every $a,b\in X$ with $a<b$ there exists $c\in A$ such that $a<c<b$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }X\text{ be a linearly ordered set and }A\subseteq X.\\
&A\text{ is order dense in }X
\Longleftrightarrow
\forall a,b\in X\;\bigl(a<b\rightarrow \exists c\in A\;(a<c<b)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }X\text{ be a linearly ordered set and }A\subseteq X.\\
&A\text{ is order dense in }X
\Longleftrightarrow
\forall a,b\in X\;\bigl(a<b\rightarrow \exists c\in A\;(a<c<b)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }X\text{ be a linearly ordered set and }A\subseteq X.\\
&A\text{ is not order dense in }X
\Longleftrightarrow
\exists a,b\in X\;\bigl(a<b\land \forall c\in A\;(c\le a\lor b\le c)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }X\text{ be a linearly ordered set and }A\subseteq X.\\
&A\text{ is not order dense in }X
\Longleftrightarrow
\exists a,b\in X\;\bigl(a<b\land \forall c\in A\;(c\le a\lor b\le c)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_predicate:
\[
\neg\operatorname{OrderDenseSubset}(A,X)\Longleftrightarrow
\exists a,b\in X\;\bigl(a<b\land \forall c\in A\;(c\le a\lor b\le c)\bigr).
\]
nodes.failure_modes: Order density fails when there is a nonempty open interval of $X$ that contains no point of $A$.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\neg\operatorname{OrderDenseSubset}(A,X)
=
\underbrace{\exists a,b\in X\;(a<b)}_{\text{nonempty interval}}
\;\land\;
\underbrace{\forall c\in A\;(c\le a\lor b\le c)}_{\text{no point of }A\text{ lies between }a\text{ and }b}.
\]
nodes.source_text: Order density is an interval-penetration property. It ignores metric distance and asks only whether every ordered gap contains a point of the subset. A subset can therefore be order dense in $X$ precisely when no nonempty open interval of $X$ is missed entirely by that subset.

nodes.name: Density of the Rational Numbers in $ $
nodes.question: State the theorem: Density of the Rational Numbers in $ $.
nodes.statement_display: For every $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, there exists $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that $a<q<b$.
nodes.statement_tex: For every $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, there exists $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that $a<q<b$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall a,b\in\mathbb{R}\;\bigl(a<b\rightarrow \exists q\in\mathbb{Q}\;(a<q<b)\bigr).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall a,b\in\mathbb{R}\;\bigl(a<b\rightarrow \exists q\in\mathbb{Q}\;(a<q<b)\bigr).
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\exists a,b\in\mathbb{R}\;\bigl(a<b\land \forall q\in\mathbb{Q}\;(q\le a\lor b\le q)\bigr).
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\exists a,b\in\mathbb{R}\;\bigl(a<b\land \forall q\in\mathbb{Q}\;(q\le a\lor b\le q)\bigr).
\]
nodes.failure_modes: The theorem would fail precisely if some nonempty real interval contained no rational number.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\neg\operatorname{OrderDenseSubset}(\mathbb{Q},\mathbb{R})
=
\underbrace{\exists a,b\in\mathbb{R}\;(a<b)}_{\text{nonempty real interval}}
\;\land\;
\underbrace{\forall q\in\mathbb{Q}\;(q\le a\lor b\le q)}_{\text{no rational lies inside}}.
\]
nodes.source_text: Rational numbers penetrate every real interval. The theorem does not say that every real number is rational; it says that rational numbers are never separated from a real interval by a positive order gap. This is the interval form of rational density in the real line.

nodes.name: Nested Interval Property
nodes.question: State the theorem: Nested Interval Property.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(I_n)$ be a sequence of nonempty closed intervals in $\mathbb{R}$. If $I_{n+1}\subseteq I_n$ for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$, then
\[
 \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} I_n\neq\varnothing.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(I_n)$ be a sequence of nonempty closed intervals in $\mathbb{R}$. If $I_{n+1}\subseteq I_n$ for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$, then
\[
 \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} I_n\neq\varnothing.
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\forall (a_n)\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;\forall (b_n)\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;\\
&\Bigl[
\bigl(\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(a_n\le b_n)\bigr)
\land
\bigl(\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;([a_{n+1},b_{n+1}]\subseteq [a_n,b_n])\bigr)
\\
&\qquad\rightarrow
\exists x\in\mathbb{R}\;\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(x\in [a_n,b_n])
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\forall (a_n)\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;\forall (b_n)\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;\\
&\Bigl[
\bigl(\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(a_n\le b_n)\bigr)
\land
\bigl(\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;([a_{n+1},b_{n+1}]\subseteq [a_n,b_n])\bigr)
\\
&\qquad\rightarrow
\exists x\in\mathbb{R}\;\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(x\in [a_n,b_n])
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\exists (a_n)\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;\exists (b_n)\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;\\
&\Bigl[
\bigl(\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(a_n\le b_n)\bigr)
\land
\bigl(\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;([a_{n+1},b_{n+1}]\subseteq [a_n,b_n])\bigr)
\\
&\qquad\land
\forall x\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists n\in\mathbb{N}\;(x\notin [a_n,b_n])
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\exists (a_n)\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;\exists (b_n)\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;\\
&\Bigl[
\bigl(\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(a_n\le b_n)\bigr)
\land
\bigl(\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;([a_{n+1},b_{n+1}]\subseteq [a_n,b_n])\bigr)
\\
&\qquad\land
\forall x\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists n\in\mathbb{N}\;(x\notin [a_n,b_n])
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.failure_modes: The theorem fails when a nested sequence of nonempty closed real intervals has no common real point.
nodes.proof_sketch:
Step 1. Analyze the endpoints of the nested intervals. By the definition of nesting, $[a_{n+1}, b_{n+1}] \subseteq [a_n, b_n]$. This implies the following ordering:
\[ a_n \le a_{n+1} \le b_{n+1} \le b_n \]
This tells us that $(a_n)$ is a $\operatorname{MonotoneIncreasingSequence}$ and $(b_n)$ is a $\operatorname{MonotoneDecreasingSequence}$.
Step 2. Establish a bound for the sequence of left endpoints. Every $b_k$ serves as an upper bound for the entire set $\{a_n : n \in \mathbb{N}\}$. To see this, if $n \le k$, then $a_n \le a_k \le b_k$. If $n > k$, then $a_n \le b_n \le b_k$. Thus, $a_n \le b_k$ for all $n, k$.
Step 3. Invoke the Least Upper Bound property. Let $A = \{a_n : n \in \mathbb{N}\}$. Since $A$ is non-empty and bounded above (by $b_1$), $x = \sup A$ exists in $\mathbb{R}$.
Step 4. Show the supremum is contained in every interval. By definition of the supremum as an upper bound, $a_n \le x$ for all $n$.
From Step 2, we know that every $b_n$ is an upper bound for $A$. By the "leastness" property of the supremum, $x \le b_n$ for all $n$.
Combining these, we have:
\[ a_n \le x \le b_n \implies x \in [a_n, b_n] \text{ for all } n \in \mathbb{N} \]
Step 5. Conclude non-empty intersection. Since $x$ is in every $I_n$, it belongs to their intersection. Thus, $\bigcap I_n$ is not empty.

nodes.proof_idea: The proof utilizes the Monotone Convergence/Least Upper Bound property to identify the supremum of the left endpoints as a point that is caught between the "rising floor" and the "falling ceiling" of the intervals.
nodes.source_text: Nested closed intervals cannot keep shrinking or relocating in a way that loses every real point. Nesting keeps the intervals aligned, nonemptiness prevents trivial collapse at each stage, and completeness supplies a real point that survives all stages at once.
